section.heading: Exceptions.
section.text: This chapter does not apply to an individual who:

para.text: Other than a certified nursing assistant, practices a health occupation, that the individual is authorized to practice under the Health Occupations Article, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Provides for the gratuitous care of friends, domestic partners, or family members;
Performs nursing assistant tasks while a nursing student enrolled in an accredited nursing program if the individual either:
Is practicing under the direct supervision of qualified faculty or preceptors; or

Successfully completed the portion of the approved nursing education program that the Board determines meets the requirements of an approved nursing assistant training program as described in Regulation .05D of this chapter;
Performs nursing assistant tasks:
While a student enrolled in an approved nursing assistant training program or approved acute care nursing assistant training program; and
While practicing under the direct supervision of qualified faculty or preceptors;
Practices as a certified nursing assistant-I for less than 4 months in accordance with 42 CFR §483.35(d);
Performs nursing tasks delegated by a registered nurse or licensed practical nurse so long as the delegated nursing tasks do not become a routine part of the individual's job duties;
Performs nursing tasks delegated by an advanced practice registered nurse in accordance with Health Occupations Article, § 8-6A-02(d), Annotated Code of Maryland; or
Performs technical tasks delegated by a licensed certified midwife in accordance with Health Occupations Article, § 8-6A-02(e), Annotated Code of Maryland.
In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Acute care nursing assistant training program” means a course of training that meets the acute care nursing assistant training program curriculum approved by the Board.
“Board” means the Maryland Board of Nursing.
“Central Repository” means the Maryland Criminal Justice Information System Central Repository of the Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services.
“Certificate” means a certificate issued by the Board to practice as a certified nursing assistant in the State.
Certified Nursing Assistant (CNA).
“Certified nursing assistant (CNA)” means an individual, regardless of title, who, for compensation and subject to regulations adopted by the Board, routinely performs:
Nursing tasks delegated by a registered nurse, licensed practical nurse, or advanced practice registered nurse; or
Technical tasks delegated by a licensed certified midwife.
“Certified nursing assistant (CNA)” does not include dialysis technician or medication technician.
Certified Nursing Assistant-I (CNA-I).
“Certified nursing assistant-I (CNA-I)” means a CNA who:
Meets the requirements set by the Board and federal law; and
May practice in any practice setting.
“Certified nursing assistant-I (CNA-I)” includes a:
CNA who was certified as a geriatric nursing assistant on March 31, 2026;
Certified medicine aide; and
CNA who is certified under Health Occupations Article, §8-6A-05(c)(5), Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Certified nursing assistant-II (CNA-II)” means a CNA who:
On March 31, 2026, was certified as a CNA but not certified as a geriatric nursing assistant; and
May not practice in a nursing facility or skilled nursing facility.

“Criminal history records check (CHRC)” means the performance of both a State criminal history records check by the Central Repository and a national criminal history records check performed by the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI).

“Criminal history record information” means information provided by the criminal history records check regarding an applicant’s criminal arrests, charges, and convictions and other dispositions of criminal charges.
“Good standing” means without an encumbrance that limits or restricts practice, such as revocation, suspension, or probation.
“Nursing assistant competency evaluation” means an examination approved by the Board that:
Determines the competency of the individual to practice as a CNA-I; and
Meets the requirements under federal law.
“Nursing assistant training program” means a course of training approved by the Board that meets the requirements under federal and State law.
“Service member” has the meaning stated in State Government Article, §9-901, Annotated Code of Maryland.

“Temporary nurse aide” means an individual who practiced as a geriatric nursing assistant without certification during the national Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) Public Health Emergency, as declared on January 31, 2020, and periodically continued by the United States Secretary of Health and Human Services, pursuant to the waiver of the requirements of 42 CFR §483.35(d) issued by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services on March 6, 2020.
“Technical task” means a routine clinical act or task that does not require clinical judgment and is performed with the supervision as specified in COMAR 10.69.02.
“Temporary nurse aide training” means an 8-hour online training and examination program offered by:
The American Health Care Association;
The National Center for Assisted Living; or
Another comparable program approved by the Board.
Fees are as follows:
Initial certification fee — $20;
Endorsement fee — $20;
Renewal fee — $40;
Verification of certification to another jurisdiction — $25;
Returned check fee — $25;
Duplicate certificate fee — $10.
Fees are not refundable.
An applicant shall submit:
A completed application to the Board on the form that the Board requires;
To a CHRC in accordance with Health Occupations Article, §8-303, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Written evidence satisfactory to the Board that the applicant has submitted to a CHRC as required by §A(2) of this regulation by submitting:
Two sets of fingerprints, as required by the Central Repository and the FBI; and
All fees required by the Central Repository and the FBI;

Any documentation requested from the applicant by the Board including, but not limited to, official certified or true test court documents and a signed, dated explanation written by the applicant, regarding the facts and circumstances, outcome, and current status of any criminal history record information received by the Board:
Under §A(2) of this regulation;
In an answer to a question on the Board’s application form; or
From any other source; and
Any additional documentation requested by the Board if the documentation received from the applicant under §A(4) of this regulation is incomplete or insufficient.

If an application is not complete when initially submitted to the Board by the applicant, the applicant shall have no longer than 12 months from the date the application is received by the Board to complete the application and provide all information and documents required in §A of this regulation.

If an applicant fails to provide to the Board a complete application and any additional documentation requested by the Board under §A of this regulation within 12 months from the date the application is received by the Board, then the application shall be considered abandoned and may be destroyed.

Once an application is deemed abandoned under §C of this regulation, an individual seeking CNA certification shall submit a new application on the form required by the Board and meet all of the requirements for CNA certification and pay the required fees that are in effect at the time of re-application.
Initial Certification. An applicant for initial nursing assistant certification shall have successfully:
Completed:
An approved nursing assistant training program;
An approved acute care nursing assistant training program;
A portion of an approved nursing education program that has been determined by the Board to meet the requirements of a nursing assistant training program set by the Board; or

Service member training and education that has been determined by the Board to be substantially equivalent to a nursing assistant training program approved by the Board in accordance with Health Occupations Article, §1-702, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
Passed a nursing assistant competency evaluation.
Certification by Endorsement. An applicant for nursing assistant certification by endorsement shall:
Hold a certificate to practice as a nursing assistant that:

Was issued by another state or territory of the United States that has certification requirements that are essentially similar to those set forth in Health Occupations Article, §8-6A-05, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
Is in good standing; and
Have successfully completed a nursing assistant training program that meets the standards for an approved nursing assistant training program set by the Board.
Any applicant for nursing assistant certification may not have:
Committed any act or omission that would be grounds for discipline or denial of certification under Health Occupations Article, §8-6A-10, Annotated Code of Maryland; or

A record of abuse, negligence, or misappropriation of a client's property or any disciplinary action taken or pending in any other state or territory of the United States against the certification of the CNA.
Nursing Education Programs.
To meet the requirements of a nursing assistant training program set by the Board, a portion of a nursing education program must include courses with content that primarily focuses on:
Nursing fundamentals;
Mental health;
Health assessment;
Nursing ethics; and
The content listed under 42 CFR §483.152(b).

If a portion of a nursing education program does not include courses with content that primarily focuses on the subject matters set forth in § D(1) of this regulation, the Board may review and approve other equivalent subject matters as meeting the requirements of a nursing assistant training program set by the Board.
A CNA-II who seeks certification as a CNA-1 shall meet the qualifications set forth in §A of this regulation.
Temporary Nurse Aides.
A temporary nurse aide applying for certification as a CNA-1 shall be deemed to have completed a nursing assistant training program approved by the Board if the temporary nurse aide:
Completed a temporary nurse aide training course;
Completed a minimum of 100 hours of on-the-job training as a temporary nurse aide that:
Met the requirements under 42 CFR §483.152(a) and 42 CFR §483.152(b)(2)—(7); and
Was not provided in a facility described under 42 CFR §483.151(b)(2)—(3); and
In addition to the minimum 100 hours of on-the-job training, worked as a temporary nurse aide competently for a minimum of 160 hours.

A temporary nurse aide whose on-the-job training did not meet all of the requirements under 42 CFR 483.152(a) and 42 CFR 483.152(b)(2)—(7) may receive supplemental training in those requirements and be deemed to meet the requirement of §F(1)(b)(i) of this regulation.
Initial Certification.
An applicant for initial certification shall:
Comply with all of the requirements under Regulation .04A of this chapter;
Pay the required fee to the Board; and
Submit to the Board one of the items in §A(2) of this regulation.
Verification Requirements.

For graduates of a nursing assistant or acute care nursing assistant training program, an application that has been signed by the administrator or faculty of the training program completed by the applicant;

For service members, documentation demonstrating successful completion of service member training and education that has been determined by the Board to be substantially equivalent to a nursing assistant training program approved by the Board;

For nursing students and graduates, an official transcript demonstrating successful completion of a portion of an approved nursing education program that the Board determines meets the requirements for a nursing assistant training program set by the Board; or

For foreign-educated nursing graduates, a copy of an evaluation of the applicant’s transcript through a Board-approved vendor demonstrating successful completion of a portion of an approved nursing education program that meets the requirements for a nursing assistant training program set by the Board.
A program coordinator or faculty of a nursing assistant or acute care nursing assistant training program:
May sign an application before graduation; and
Shall submit written verification, as required by the Board, of program completion to the Board after 5 business days of the applicant’s program completion.

An applicant shall submit an application for initial certification after 24 months of the date of completion of an approved nursing assistant training program, an approved acute care nursing assistant training program, or portion of an approved nursing education program.

If an applicant fails to meet § A(4) of this regulation, an applicant still seeking certification shall take or re-take an approved nursing assistant training program or approved acute care nursing assistant training program and comply with §A(4) of this regulation.
Certification by Endorsement. An applicant for nursing assistant certification by endorsement shall:
Comply with all of the requirements under Regulation .04A of this chapter;
Pay the required fee; and
Submit the following verifications directly to the Board from the appropriate authority:
A certificate to practice as a nursing assistant in good standing from another state or territory with certification requirements that are essentially similar to the requirements in this State; and
A certificate of completion from a nursing assistant training program in that state or territory that meets the standards for an approved nursing assistant training program set by the Board.
Every applicant shall attest on the application whether the applicant has:
Committed any act or omission that would be grounds for discipline or denial of certification; and

A record of abuse, negligence, or misappropriation of a client’s property or any disciplinary action taken or pending in any state or territory of the United States against the certification of the applicant.
A CNA-II who seeks certification as a CNA-I shall:
Submit an application to the Board on the form required by the Board; and
Comply with §§A(1)(c) and A(3)—(5) of this regulation.
A temporary nurse aide applying for certification as a CNA-1 shall:
Comply with §A of this regulation; and
Ensure that the administrator, director of nursing, or human resources representative of the applicant’s employer submits to the Board an attestation, on the form required by the Board, that:
Is completed by the:
Applicant;
Registered Nurse instructor or supervisor of the applicant during employment; and
Administrator or director of nursing for the employer; and
Verifies that the applicant satisfies the requirements of Regulation .05F of this chapter.
An individual is eligible to sit for a nursing assistant competency evaluation if the individual:
Meets the education requirements set forth in Regulation .05A(1) of this chapter; or
Must retake a nursing assistant competency evaluation in order to renew or reinstate a certification in accordance with Regulation .10C(2)(a) of this chapter.

Except for service members, an individual shall sit for a nursing assistant competency evaluation after 24 months of meeting the educational requirements set forth in Regulation .05A(1) of this chapter.

An individual who fails to sit for a nursing assistant competency evaluation after 24 months of meeting the educational qualifications must re-take an approved nursing assistant training program or approved acute care nursing assistant training program in order to be eligible to sit for a nursing assistant competency evaluation.
Re-testing.
An individual who fails to obtain a passing score on the first examination may retake a nursing assistant competency evaluation three additional times.
An individual shall wait at least 45 calendar days from the date of the prior evaluation before retaking a nursing assistant competency evaluation.

An individual who fails to obtain a passing score after taking a nursing assistant competency evaluation four times must complete a new approved nursing assistant training program or approved acute care nursing assistant training program before retaking a nursing assistant competency evaluation.
The Board may issue a temporary practice certificate to an applicant who:
Has met the appropriate certification requirements to the satisfaction of the Board;
Does not have a criminal record; and
Has not been the subject of a health professional disciplinary action in this State or another jurisdiction.
Unless the Board suspends or revokes a temporary practice certificate, the temporary practice certificate expires 90 calendar days after the date of issuance.
A temporary practice certificate may be extended up to an additional 90 calendar days if the applicant is awaiting the completion of the criminal history records check.
The Board may not issue a certificate to an applicant until the Board has:
Received and reviewed the criminal history record information of the applicant; and
Approved the application.

Unless subject to discipline, the Board shall issue a certificate to each applicant who meets all the requirements of this chapter and Health Occupations Article, §§8-6A-05 and 8-6A-06, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Expiration.
Unless renewed for an additional two-year term, and except as otherwise provided in §A(2) of this regulation, a certificate expires in accordance with the following schedule:
The certificate of a certificate holder with an odd-numbered birth year expires on the 28th day of the certificate holder’s birth month in an odd-numbered year; and
The certificate of a certificate holder with an even-numbered birth year expires on the 28th day of the certificate holder’s birth month in an even-numbered year.
A certificate shall not expire within 180 calendar days of the date of issuance.
Renewal Notice.
At least 3 months before a certificate expires, the Board shall send the certificate holder a renewal notice by either:
First-class mail to the last known address of the certificate holder; or
Electronic means to the last known electronic address of the certificate holder.
The renewal notice shall provide:
The date on which the certificate holder’s certificate is scheduled to expire;

The date by which the Board must receive a renewal application in order for a certificate to remain active until the renewal application submitted by the certificate holder is processed by the Board, in accordance with State Government Article, §10-226(b), Annotated Code of Maryland;
The amount of the renewal fee; and
If the certificate holder is required to submit to a CHRC in accordance with §D of this regulation:
Notice that the certificate holder must submit to a CHRC in order to renew the certificate; and
Written instructions for how to complete a CHRC.
It is the responsibility of the certificate holder to:
Be aware of the expiration date of the certificate; and
Renew the certificate before it expires.
Failure of a certificate holder to receive the renewal notice sent by the Board does not relieve the certificate holder of the responsibility to renew a certificate before it expires.
Renewal Requirements.
The Board shall renew a certificate if the individual applying for renewal:
Is otherwise entitled to be certified;
Submits a complete application for renewal on the form required by the Board;
Pays the renewal fee;

Provides satisfactory evidence, as required by the Board, of16 hours of active employment, for compensation, as a nursing assistant within the 2-year period immediately preceding the date of expiration or meet the requirements under §C(2) of this regulation; and
If required to submit to a CHRC in accordance with § D of this regulation, complies with the requirements of Regulation .04A of this chapter.
If the individual applying for renewal does not have 16 hours of active employment as a nursing assistant within the 2-year period immediately preceding the date of individual the individual shall:
If a CNA-I, take and pass a nursing assistant competency evaluation; or
If a CNA-II, complete a nursing assistant training program..
Schedule for CHRCs. CHRCs for renewal applicants shall be scheduled according to the certificate holder’s birth month as follows:
In 2025 — all applicants with an August or September birth month;
In 2026 — all applicants with a September or October birth month;
In 2027 — all applicants with an October or November birth month;
In 2028 — all applicants with a November or December birth month;
In 2029 — all applicants with a December or January birth month;
In 2030 — all applicants with a January or February birth month;
In 2031 — all applicants with a February or March birth month;
In 2032 — all applicants with a March or April birth month;
In 2033 — all applicants with an April or May birth month;
In 2034 — all applicants with a May or June birth month;
In 2035 — all applicants with a June or July birth month; and
In 2036 — all applicants with a July or August birth month.
Grace Period. A certificate holder has a 30 calendar day grace period beyond the expiration date of the certificate to renew the certificate.

The Board shall reinstate the certificate of a certificate holder who has failed to renew the certificate for any reason, if the certificate holder meets the renewal requirements in Health Occupations Article, §8-6A-08, Annotated Code of Maryland, and Regulation .10 of this chapter.
An applicant for administrative reinstatement who has failed to renew the certificate for a period of 1 year or more shall submit to a CHRC before the certificate is reinstated.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Acute care nursing assistant training program” means a course of training that meets the acute care nursing assistant training program curriculum approved by the Board.
“Board” means the Maryland Board of Nursing.
“Certified Nursing Assistant (CNA)” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.39.01.02B.
Certified Nursing Assistant-I (CNA-I).
“Certified nursing assistant-I (CNA-I)” means a CNA who:
Meets the requirements set by the Board and federal law; and
May practice in any practice setting.
“Certified nursing assistant-I (CNA-I)” includes a:
CNA who was certified as a geriatric nursing assistant on March 31, 2026;
Certified medicine aide; and
CNA who is certified under Health Occupations Article, §8-6A-05(c)(5), Annotated Code of Maryland.

“Classroom laboratory training” means instruction and demonstration, on an individual or a mannequin, of patient care skills by an instructor and return demonstration of competence in these skills by the student.
“Clinical facility” means a setting where clients are physically present and that provides facilities for supervised clinical training.
“Direct supervision” means that the registered nurse or licensed practical nurse is physically present while the student demonstrates patient care skills.

“Distance education” means a formal education process in which the delivery of instruction occurs through the use of telecommunication technologies because the students and instructors are in different places.
“Hybrid training program” means a nursing assistant training program that provides:
Didactic instruction through distance education;
On-site classroom laboratory training; and
Supervised clinical training.
“Licensed Practical Nurse (LPN)” means an individual who:
Is licensed by the Board to practice licensed practical nursing; or
Has a multistate licensure privilege to practice licensed practical nursing.
“Multistate licensure privilege” has the meaning set forth in COMAR 10.27.01.01B.
“Nursing assistant competency evaluation” means an examination approved by the Board that:
Determines the competency of the individual to practice as a CNA-I; and
Meets the requirements under federal law.
“Nursing assistant training program” means a course of training approved by the Board that meets the requirements under federal and State law.
“Program completion” means that the student:
Completed and demonstrated competency in the program objectives and performance indicators during the didactic, laboratory, and clinical portions of the training program; and
Passed the final written examination.
“Program provider” means an individual, organization, facility, or educational institution that conducts a nursing assistant training program or an acute care nursing assistant training program.
“Registered nurse (RN)” means an individual who:
Is licensed by the Board to practice registered nursing; or
Has a multistate licensure privilege to practice registered nursing.

“Supervised clinical training” means training in person at a clinical facility in which the student demonstrates knowledge and skills while performing tasks on a client under the supervision of an RN or an LPN.
“Traditional program” means a training program that provides on-site didactic instruction, classroom laboratory training, and supervised clinical training.
“Training program” means a nursing assistant training program or an acute care nursing assistant training program.
“Unencumbered” means without limitation or restriction on practice, such as revocation, suspension, or probation.

“Written agreement” means a contract between a program provider and a clinical facility outlining the terms required, by both the program and the facility, for supervised clinical training of students.
Approval Requirements.
All training programs shall be approved by:
The Board; and
The Maryland Higher Education Commission (MHEC) in accordance with Health Occupations Article, §8-6A-14(a), Annotated Code of Maryland and COMAR 13B.01.01.03.
Public High School Training Programs. If the training program is offered by a public high school, the training program shall be approved by the State Board of Education.
The approval process shall be completed before the training program is offered.
Application. At least 120 days before the proposed start date of the training program, a program provider seeking initial approval of a training program shall:
Submit a complete application on the form required by the Board;
Demonstrate that the training program meets all of the applicable requirements in this chapter; and
Provide any additional information requested by the Board during the Board’s review of the application.
Board Action.
Following receipt of a complete application, the Board’s professional staff may conduct a survey visit and submit a survey visit report to the Board.
The Board shall:
Approve a training program that meets all of the applicable requirements of this chapter; or
Deny approval of a training program that does not meet all of the applicable requirements of this chapter.
Term. Board approval of a training program is valid for 2 years.
Ongoing Responsibilities.
A training program shall obtain approval from the Board before making any substantial changes to the program.
Substantial changes include:
Changes to faculty;
Significant changes to curriculum, including an increase or decrease in program hours; and
Removal or addition of clinical facilities or classroom locations.

A training program shall submit to the Board an annual report, on the form required by the Board, that includes statistics on enrollments, program completions, attrition rates, and nursing assistant competency evaluation results.
Nursing Assistant Competency Evaluation Performance.

A training program shall maintain an average pass rate of 90 percent of the average pass rate for all states using the applicable Board-approved vendors for the nursing assistant competency evaluation.
The Board shall annually review the performance of graduates from each training program on the nursing assistant competency evaluation.
Equal Opportunity Statement. The training program provider shall have a statement of equal opportunity.

A training program offered by a nursing facility or skilled nursing facility shall not charge a fee for any portion of the program, including textbooks and other required course materials, in accordance with 42 CFR §483.152(c).
Faculty Requirements.
Each course instructor and program coordinator shall have:
An active, unencumbered license to practice as a Registered Nurse or a multistate licensure privilege to practice as a Registered Nurse in the State;
A minimum of 2 years of nursing experience; and
Completed a course in teaching adults or have experience in teaching adults or supervising nursing assistants.

Long-Term Care Facility Experience Requirements. Either the program coordinator or all course instructors shall have at least 1 year of experience in the provision of long-term care facility services, which may include:
Providing direct client care or supervisory oversight in a long-term care facility or unit, including in a geriatrics department, chronic care hospital, or other long-term care setting; and
Teaching a nursing assistant training program that complies with federal law.
Course Instructors.
Course instructors in training programs in public secondary schools in the State shall also meet the requirements set forth in COMAR 13A.12.02.02.
The course instructor is responsible for:
Participating in the development and evaluation of the training program, including the acquisition of learning resources and instructional materials;
Implementing the training program and ensuring course objectives are met;

Supervising classroom, laboratory, and clinical learning experiences, including direct supervision of a student the first time a student performs a patient care skill during a clinical learning experience and until the student demonstrates proficiency in that skill; and
Evaluating student performance during didactic, clinical, and laboratory training.
Program Coordinator.
Each training program shall have a program coordinator who:
Provides general supervision of the training of nursing assistants; and
Maintains responsibility for the overall operation of the program.
Except as provided in §C(3) of this regulation, the program coordinator may also be a course instructor.
In a facility-based program, the facility’s director of nursing may be the program coordinator but may not perform any actual training.
Supplemental Personnel.
Other health professional personnel may supplement the course instructor, including, but not limited to:
Registered nurses in accordance with §D(3) of this regulation;
Licensed practical nurses in accordance with §D(4) of this regulation;
Pharmacists;
Dietitians;
Social workers;
Sanitarians;
Fire safety experts;
Nursing home administrators;
Gerontologists;
Psychologists;
Physical and occupational therapists;
Activities specialists;
Speech/language/hearing therapists; and
Resident rights experts.
Supplemental personnel shall have at least 1 year of experience in their respective field.
A registered nurse functioning as supplemental personnel shall:
Hold a current, unencumbered license in the State or multistate licensure privilege as a registered nurse; and
Have had at least 1 year of direct client care experience as a registered nurse.
A licensed practical nurse functioning as supplemental personnel shall:
Hold a current, unencumbered license in the State or multistate licensure privilege as a practical nurse; and
Have had at least 1 year of direct client care experience as a licensed practical nurse.
Other supplemental personnel providing instruction shall provide instruction under the supervision of the course instructo.
General Facility Requirements. All classroom, laboratory, and clinical facilities used by a training program:
Shall be approved by the Board;
Shall be approved or accredited by the appropriate agency;
May not be on conditional or provisional approval status; and
May not meet any of the factors set forth in 42 CFR §483.151(b)(2)—(3).
Classroom and Laboratory Facilities. Classroom and laboratory facilities shall include:
Adequate space for privacy of faculty-student conferences; and
Space for equipment and instructional materials.
Resources.
All instructional learning resources and materials shall:
Be accessible and relevant to the role of the CNA;
Have a publication date not older than 5 years from the current year; and
Be written at an 8th grade level.
Laboratory Equipment.
Laboratory equipment, at a minimum, shall include materials to support the following classroom activities:
Infection control;
Basic nursing skills;
Personal care skills; and
Basic restorative services;
The Board shall maintain on its website a list of the specific equipment required for laboratory facilities.
Clinical Facilities.
Clinical facilities shall:
Have a sufficient number and variety of clients to provide training experiences for all students to achieve the stated objectives;
Have a sufficient number of registered nurses and other nursing personnel to ensure safe and continuous care of clients; and
Conform with accepted standards of nursing care and practice.
There shall be a minimum of 1 course instructor for each 8 students in the clinical area.
Written Agreements.
Except for facility-based programs, a training program shall have a written agreement with a clinical facility for student training experiences.
The written agreement developed in accordance with §D(3)(a) of this regulation shall:
Be developed jointly with the clinical facility and the training program;
Be reviewed periodically by the clinical facility and the training program;
Include a provision for adequate notice of termination;
Specify the responsibility of the training program to the clinical facility and the responsibility of the clinical facility to the training program; and
Identify the functions and responsibilities of the parties involved.
Program Hours.
A training program shall provide a minimum of 100 hours of instruction.
Of the 100 hours, a minimum of:

60 hours shall be devoted to didactic instruction and classroom laboratory training, with at least 16 hours of in-person laboratory training in the pre-clinical curriculum listed in §B(3) of this regulation; and
40 hours shall be devoted to supervised clinical training in a clinical facility.

A training program shall have at least 16 of the 40 hours of supervised clinical training in the provision of long-term care facility services or caring for the elderly or chronically ill of any age, which shall be performed under direct supervision.
Curriculum Content.
The curriculum for a training program shall include the subject matters and skills listed in 42 CFR §483.152(b).
The curriculum for an acute care nursing assistant training program shall include:
The subject matters listed in 42 CFR §483.152(b); and
All basic skills required of a nursing assistant in any acute care practice setting, including:
Caring for patients with acute and chronic medical conditions in a hospital facility;
Observing and reporting changes in patient condition, including potential emergencies and safety issues, to the licensed nurse;
Transport of specimens;
Transport of medications;
Point of care testing;
Specimen collecting;
Equipment management;
Time management in the acute care setting;
De-escalation of assertive or aggressive patients and family members; and
Electronic documentation of care and basic computer skills.
The pre-clinical curriculum of a training program shall include the following topics:
Role of the CNA;
Infection control;
Safety and emergency procedures, including the Heimlich maneuver;
Communication and interpersonal skills;
Promoting clients’ independence; and
Legal and ethical considerations, including respecting clients’ rights.
A training program shall provide instruction in the pre-clinical curriculum before a student has any direct contact with a client.
Each training program shall identify the unit objectives and performance indicators for each of the areas in §B of this regulation in its training criteria.
Laboratory Instruction and Clinical Learning Experience.
Each training program shall provide laboratory instruction in and require students to demonstrate clinical competence in performing skills consistent with the subjects under §B of this regulation.

A training program shall ensure that students may not perform any services during their clinical learning experience for which they have not trained and been found proficient by the course instructor.
Each training program shall have an objective final written examination that measures achievement of the performance indicators.
To successfully complete the training program, each student shall:
Complete the required minimum hours provided in Regulation .06A of this chapter;
Demonstrate the ability to perform nursing functions with clients of any age and with various levels of physical, sensory, cognitive, and emotional ability;
Achieve a passing score of at least 75 percent on the written final examination; and
Demonstrate competence in meeting the objectives and performance indicators during didactic, laboratory, and clinical instruction.
The training program shall provide the following to students who complete the program:
A certificate of completion using the form required by the Board; and
Registration information for a nursing assistant competency evaluation.
A program coordinator or faculty of a training program:
May sign a student’s application for certification as a nursing assistant before graduation; and
Shall submit to the Board written verification, as required by the Board, of each student’s program completion within 5 business days of program completion.
Expiration. Unless renewed, Board approval of a training program expires 2 years from the last date of approval.
Renewal application. At least 120 days before the date of expiration of approval, a training program shall submit:
A complete application on the form required by the Board; and
All accompanying documentation required by the application or requested by the Board.

Survey Visit. Following receipt of a renewal application from a training program, the Board’s professional staff shall conduct an announced or unannounced on-site survey visit and submit a survey visit report to the Board.
Board Action. The Board shall:
Renew approval of a training program for another 2-year term if the training program meets the applicable requirements of this chapter; or
Initiate withdrawal of approval proceedings in accordance with Regulation .09 of this chapter if the training program fails to meet the applicable requirements of this chapter.
Enrollment. A training program that fails to renew its approval:
Shall be listed on nonrenewed status in the Board’s records; and
May not enroll any new students to the program following the expiration of approval.
Notice of Deficiencies.
If the Board determines that there is a basis for withdrawal of approval under §C of this regulation, the Board shall issue a written notice of deficiencies to the training program.
The notice of deficiencies shall notify the training program of the:
Requirements with which the program has not complied;
Reasons for the Board’s determination; and
Requirement to submit a written plan of correction in accordance with §B of this regulation.
Written Plan of Correction.
A training program shall submit a written plan of correction after 30 calendar days of the date of the notice of deficiencies unless granted an extension by the Board for good cause.
The written plan of correction shall:
Address all deficiencies noted by the Board; and
Include dates of completion for correcting the deficiencies.
The Board shall review the plan of correction and may approve or modify the dates of completion for correcting deficiencies.
The training program shall correct the deficiencies within the time frame approved by the Board.
Grounds for Withdrawal. The Board may withdraw approval of a training program that:
Fails to comply with any of the applicable requirements of this chapter;
Fails to correct deficiencies within the time frame approved by the Board;
Enrolls new students after expiration of approval; or
Has a graduate pass rate of less than 90 percent of the pass rate for all states using the applicable Board-approved vendors for a nursing assistant competency evaluation.
Procedure for Withdrawal.
Before withdrawing approval of a training program under §C of this regulation, the Board shall:
Notify the program of its intent to withdraw approval; and
Give the program an opportunity to request an evidentiary hearing before the Board.
The notice of intent to withdraw approval and hearing shall comply with the Administrative Procedure Act.

Mandatory Withdrawal. Notwithstanding any other section of this regulation, the Board shall withdraw approval of a training program upon receipt of notice from the Office of Healthcare Quality that the facility is prohibited from holding a training program pursuant 42 CFR §483.151(f)(1) or 42 CFR §483.151(f)(3).

Reinstatement. A training program whose approval was withdrawn may apply for reinstatement of approval by submitting a reinstatement application demonstrating that the program meets all of the applicable requirements of this chapter.
When a training program provider considers closing a program, the training program provider shall:
Notify the Board in writing of:
The intended closing date; and
The arrangements for custody and safeguarding of student and graduate records; and
Continue the program until the committed class schedule of currently enrolled students is completed.
Custody of Student Records.

If the training program provider is a facility or educational institution that will continue to function, the facility or educational institution shall assume responsibility for the student and graduate records of the closed training program.

Except as provided in §B(1) of this regulation, a training program provider that is required to obtain approval from the Maryland Higher Education Commission (MHEC) shall file student and graduate records, or legible copies of those records, with MHEC in accordance with Education Article, §11-401, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A training program provider that is not required to obtain MHEC approval shall reach a mutual agreement with the Board about the disposition of student records.
Scope. This regulation applies to hybrid nursing assistant training programs.
Approval. The Board shall approve a hybrid training program that meets all of the requirements of this regulation.
Prerequisite Qualifications. A program seeking initial approval as a hybrid training program shall:
Comply with the requirements set forth in Regulation .02 of this chapter;
Be approved by the Board as a traditional training program for at least 2 years before the program applies for initial approval as a hybrid training program; and
Comply with all requirements set forth under this chapter and be in good standing with the Board at the time that the program applies for initial approval as a hybrid training program.
Application and Approval Requirements.
A training program seeking initial approval as a hybrid training program shall apply for approval on a form required by the Board.
To be eligible for Board approval, a hybrid training program shall:
Host sessions no longer than 4 hours per session;
Specify the technology platform to be used for each content area of the curriculum;
Conduct lab and clinical training:
In person with a course instructor who meets the requirements under Regulation .04 of this chapter; and
No later than 2 weeks after the completion of didactic instruction;
Require test sessions to be conducted in person;
Have a policy, which is provided to the Board as a part of the application, for:
Admissions, including determining whether a student has sufficient access to a computer and learning resources;
Orienting students to online modules;
Providing troubleshooting assistance for enrolled students; and
In the case of technology failure, an alternative method of instruction and notification to the Board of the technology failure and the use of the alternative method of instruction;
Have sufficient mechanisms for:
Requiring the use of a personal identification number that confirms the identity of the students and instructor;
Ensuring that all students have demonstrated competency on all virtual education content;
Tracking and auditing active student participation in online instruction;
Confirming student identity during and after completion of online modules and live sessions; and
Safeguarding appropriate security measures;
Provide the Board with access rights to the program to verify program compliance and monitor distance learning sessions; and
Provide the Board with statistics about the performance of students in the program, including:
Student exam pass rates;
The rate at which students repeat each module of the program; and
Any other information requested by the Board regarding student participation in and
completion of the program.
Reapproval. An approved hybrid training program shall apply for renewal of approval in accordance with Regulation .08 of this chapter.
Withdrawal of Approval. The Board may withdraw approval of a hybrid training program in accordance with Regulation .09 of this chapter.

53:9 26-016-E (Regulations .01 — .11) EMERGENCY ACTION IN EFFECT 4/3/2026 —9/30/2026
Effective date: April 30, 2001 (28:8 Md. R. 761)
Regulation .02B amended effective July 2, 2018 (45:13 Md. R. 667)
Regulation .05A amended effective July 2, 2018 (45:13 Md. R. 667)

Chapter revised as an emergency provision effective April 3, 2026 (53:9 Md. R. 411)

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Board” means the Maryland Board of Nursing.
“Certificate” means a certificate issued by the Board to practice as a nursing assistant in this State.
“Certified medicine aide (CMA)” means an individual certified in good standing as an active CNA who:
Satisfies the requirements of COMAR 10.39.03.02; and
Is further certified by the Board to accept delegation of administration of certain medications.
Certified Nursing Assistant (CNA).
“Certified nursing assistant (CNA)” means an individual, regardless of title, who, for compensation and subject to regulations adopted by the Board, routinely performs:
Nursing tasks delegated by a registered nurse, licensed practical nurse, or advanced practice registered nurse; or
Technical tasks delegated by a licensed certified midwife.
“Certified nursing assistant (CNA)” does not include dialysis technician or medication technician.
Certified Nursing Assistant-I (CNA-I).
“Certified nursing assistant-I (CNA-I)” means a CNA who:
Meets the requirements set by the Board and federal law; and
May practice in any practice setting.
“Certified nursing assistant-I (CNA-I)” includes a:
CNA who was certified as a geriatric nursing assistant on March 31, 2026;
Certified medicine aide; and
CNA who is certified pursuant to § 8-6A-05(c)(5) of the Health Occupations Article, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Certified nursing assistant-II (CNA-II)” means a CNA who:
On March 31, 2026, was certified as a CNA but not certified as a geriatric nursing assistant; and
May not practice in a nursing facility or skilled nursing facility.
“Controlled schedule drugs” means drugs listed as Schedule I, II, III, IV, or V controlled dangerous substances under Criminal Law Article, §§5-402 — 5-406, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Licensed practical nurse (LPN)” has the meaning stated in Health Occupations Article, §8-101, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Registered nurse (RN)” has the meaning stated in Health Occupations Article, §8-101, Annotated Code of Maryland.
An applicant for certification as a CMA shall provide evidence satisfactory to the Board of meeting the following requirements:
Current certification by the Board as an active CNA-1 in good standing;
Current employment and work experience as required by the Board in a setting acceptable to the Board, including:
Current employment and 2 years of full-time experience as a CNA-1 in a facility licensed under 42 CFR Part 483 et seq.; or
Current employment and 1 year of full-time experience as a CNA-I in a facility licensed in Maryland under COMAR 10.07.02; and
Successful completion as evidenced by a certificate of completion from a Board-approved CMA training program.

The community college shall submit verification of successful completion of the CMA training program to the Board within 5 business days after completion of the program in a manner prescribed by the Board.
Before renewal of certification as a CMA, and subject to the requirements in Health Occupations Article, §8-6A-08, Annotated Code of Maryland, and COMAR 10.39.01.09, the applicant shall:
Successfully complete a Board-approved 8-hour medicine aide continuing education program as required by Regulation .03B of this chapter; and
Provide satisfactory evidence of completion of 100 hours of practice as a certified medicine aide in the 2-year period preceding the date of renewal.

section.heading: Incorporation by Reference.
section.text: In this chapter, the following documents are incorporated by reference to the extent that they are referenced in this chapter:

para.text: The ASTM International Standards on Amusement Rides and Devices: F2374-22 Standard Practice for Design, Manufacture, Operation, and Maintenance of Inflatable Amusement Devices (2022) (ASTM F2374-22), excluding Sections 5.5.5.2(4), 5.12.7.2(2), 5.16.6, 7.5.4.1 reference to 7.9, 7.5.5.3(2), 7.7.4, 7.9, 7.10 & 7.11.1; and
The National Electrical Code, ANSI/NFPA #70-2020, which is incorporated by reference in COMAR 20.50.02.02.
In addition to terms defined in Business Regulation Article, §3-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, in this chapter the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“ASTM” means ASTM International, originally known as the American Society for Testing and Materials.
“Attendant” means a person assigned to assist the operator in attending to the safety needs of users.
“Certificate of inspection of an inflatable amusement attraction” means a certificate issued by the Commissioner of Labor and Industry.
“Exit means a doorway or other opening affording safe and unobstructed egress.
“Fire official” means the authority having jurisdiction in the locality over matters relating to fire prevention and explosion.
“Inflatable amusement attraction” means an air-supported amusement attraction that:
Incorporates a structural and mechanical system; and
Uses a high strength fabric or film that achieves its strength, shape, and stability by tensioning from internal air pressure.

“Inflatable Amusement Attraction Daily Inspection and Daily Pre-Opening Checklist” means the Commissioner of Labor and Industry’s form to be completed by the owner prior to the operation of an inflatable amusement attraction.

“Operator” means a person who is trained and who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards involving the inflatable amusement attraction operation, and has the authority to control and eliminate any hazards.
“Owner” means a person, the State or a political subdivision of the State that owns an inflatable amusement attraction, or if the amusement attraction is leased, the lessee.
“Qualified person” means a person who has successfully demonstrated the ability to solve or resolve problems related to the subject matter or the work:
By possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing; or
By extensive knowledge, training, and experience.
“Permanently erected inflatable amusement attraction” means an inflatable amusement attraction erected in the same location for more than 30 consecutive days.
“Rated capacity” means a capacity:
Established by the manufacturer for the normal loading and operation of an inflatable amusement attraction by weight or number of users; or
If the manufacturer has not established a capacity, as established by the Commissioner after inspection.
“Safety work order” means a written demand issued by an authorized representative of the Commissioner to perform work that has potential safety impact if not corrected.

“Special inflated amusement structure” means a fun house, dark ride, walk through, haunted house, or any other similar inflated amusement attraction that uses air pressure to support the walls and roof of the structure.
“Sustained wind speed” means wind speed determined by averaging observed values over a two-minute period.
User.
“User” means any person who is a participant in the use of an inflatable amusement attraction as defined by the manufacturer’s specifications.
“User” does not include employees of the inflatable amusement attraction owner while engaged in the duties of their employment.
“Wind gust” means a rapid fluctuation of wind speed determined by averaging observed values over a two-minute period.
For an inflatable amusement attraction manufactured after January 1, 2026, in addition to the requirements of this chapter, the requirements of ASTM F2374-22 shall apply.
For an inflatable amusement attraction manufactured before January 1, 2026, in addition to the requirements of this chapter, ASTM F2374-22 shall apply except Sections 5.5.5, 5.5.5.1, 5.13, and 6.1.3.
Each owner shall obtain the manufacturer’s operations manual and shall:
Read and become familiar with the contents of the manufacturer’s recommended operating instructions and specifications;
Keep the manufacturer’s operations manual on location with the inflatable amusement attraction; and
Provide a copy of the manufacturer’s operations manual, in English, to the Commissioner on request.
The owner shall set up and operate an inflatable amusement attraction in accordance with the following:
Owner requirements for setting up inflatables amusement attractions.
An inflatable amusement attraction shall not be operated when the maximum sustained wind speed is 15 mph or greater; or
When there are wind gusts lasting 3 seconds or longer with wind speed of 25 mph or greater in the immediate area where the inflatable attraction is being operated.
Anchorage points installed near an entrance or exit of an inflatable amusement attraction shall be connected in such a manner as to minimize the potential for tripping, abrasions, or other injuries;
Blowers and inflation tubes shall be positioned to prevent kinks or bends in the inflation tube; and
Inflatable amusement attractions shall be anchored or secured in accordance with the manufacturer’s specifications or the following requirements:
The on-ground anchor weight used for inflatable amusement attractions shall be at least 75 pounds for bounce-type rides;
The on-ground anchor weight for slide-type rides over 15 feet tall shall be at least 500 pounds for each installed anchor position;
Anchors shall be used at all of the manufacturer’s required positions at all times for both indoor and outdoor use;
Each anchor must be:
At least 1 inch in diameter; and
Solid in composition;
Anchors can be straight stakes, screw stakes, ground weights, or sandbag ground anchors, and must comply with the following:
If straight stakes are used, they must be at least 36 inches in length with at least 75 percent or more of the length in the ground; and
The ends of the stakes should be covered to prevent a tripping hazard;
Tie-downs should be attached to a secure device or permanent structure and attached so that they cannot slip off the anchor during use;
Tie-downs should not be attached to motor vehicles;
Tie-downs should comply with manufacturer’s specifications or have a minimum tensile strength of 3,700 pounds or 370 pounds safe working load; and
Permanently erected inflatable amusement attractions shall comply with the following:
The manufacturer’s specifications;
The requirements of ASTM F2374-22.5.6.9.1;
The requirements in §B(1)—(4) of this regulation; and
If erected with stakes or bolts, the stakes or bolts shall be inspected prior to each operation to ensure:
Secure anchorage;
No corrosion of anchorage; and
No debris near anchorage.
Weather conditions and forecast for the zip code area where the inflatable amusement attraction is operated shall be:
Set-up monitoring.
Monitored prior to set up of the inflatable amusement attraction; and
Recorded in a written log as provided for in Regulation .12(D)(1)(b) of this regulation;
Monitored periodically during operations using a National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration weather radio application or source similar in reliability.
Wind speeds shall be monitored prior to the set up of an inflatable amusement attraction and periodically during operation using an anemometer or other reliable method.
Operator, Attendant, and User Operating Requirements.
For an inflatable amusement attraction less than 15 feet in height, the owner shall provide the appropriate number of operators and attendants as specified by the manufacturer.

For an inflatable amusement attraction over 15 feet in height, the owner shall follow the manufacturer’s specifications as to the appropriate number of operators and attendants, but a minimum of one operator and one attendant shall be provided.
For an inflatable amusement attraction over 15 feet in height, the owner shall follow the manufacturer’s specifications as to maximum number of users.
If the manufacturer’s specifications do not provide the number of users, there shall be no more than two users on the attraction and two users in a queue at any one time.
General Owner Obligations.
An owner shall comply with ASTM F2374-22.7.4.
The owner shall ensure that the amusement attraction is positioned at a location so that the operator has a clear view of the user loading and unloading areas.
The owner shall ensure that a designated operator shall operate only one inflatable amusement attraction at a time.
Operators.
The owner shall ensure each inflatable amusement attraction:
Is under the direct control of an operator who has been trained in the operation of the assigned inflatable amusement attraction; and
Has the appropriate number of operators in the appropriate locations as provided for by the manufacturer’s specifications.
The owner shall ensure that a designated operator:
Does not operate any inflatable amusement attraction while under the influence of drugs or alcohol;
Knows the use and function of all operating controls, signal systems, and safety devices applicable to the inflatable amusement attraction;
Knows the proper user loading sequence and capacity of the particular inflatable amusement attraction being operated;
Remains stationed at the inflatable amusement attraction during operation;
Has control of the inflatable amusement attraction at all times;
Enforces applicable restrictions; and
Has no other distractions from the operator’s responsibilities.
Operator and Attendant Identification.
An owner shall issue to each operator and attendant identification that is highly visible.
The identification shall clearly indicate the following:
The title, that is, operator or attendant;
Either the name of the operator or attendant or an identification number; and
The name of the inflatable amusement attraction company.
Before issuing any identification, the owner shall train the operator and the attendant in accordance with §I of this regulation.
Operator and Attendant Training.
An owner shall comply with the requirements of ASTM F2374-22.7.8 regarding operator and attendant training;
An operator or attendant shall be trained on how to monitor the local weather including assessing the wind speeds using an anemometer or other reliable method; and

The owner of an inflatable amusement attraction shall ensure that the Inflatable Amusement Attraction Daily Inspection and Daily Pre-Opening Checklist is properly filled out and signed at the completion of the daily pre-opening program.
Daily Inspection Program.
An owner shall comply with the requirements of ASTM F2374-22.7.7, except §7.7.4.
Worn and damaged components shall be repaired or replaced immediately and before operation.

The owner of an inflatable amusement attraction shall ensure that the Inflatable Amusement Attraction Daily Inspection and Daily Pre-Opening Checklist is properly filled out and signed at the completion of the daily pre-opening program.
A copy of the Inflatable Amusement Attraction Daily Inspection and Daily Pre-Opening Checklist shall be provided to the Commissioner on request.
Injury Reporting.
Whenever a death, in-patient hospitalization, amputation, compound fracture, broken tooth, or loss of an eye occurs during the use of an inflatable amusement attraction:
The person who directly controls the operation of the inflatable amusement attraction shall immediately close the inflatable amusement attraction until it has been inspected by the Commissioner; and
The amusement owner shall report orally or in writing to the Commissioner within 24 hours:
Date and time of the incident;
Name, address, and telephone number of the injured person and parent or guardian information if the injured person is a minor;
Age of the injured person;
Name and manufacturer of the inflatable amusement attraction;
Description of the injury;
Description of events related to the incident; and
Name, address, and phone number of the inflatable amusement attraction operator and attendants.

If an owner is unaware of the condition of a member of the public injured during the operation of an inflatable amusement attraction who was transported away from the premises by an emergency response vehicle, the owner shall report verbally to the Commissioner within 24 hours the information required in §A(1)(b) of this regulation.
Injury Recording.

In addition to the reporting requirements of §A of this regulation, all owners shall maintain an injury log of all injuries to the public that occur during use of inflatable amusement attractions that require medical treatment by a physician other than one-time first aid and does not include:
An injury that does not ordinarily require medical treatment, such as a minor burn, splinter, contusion, scratch, or cut not requiring stitches; or
A diagnostic procedure, including examination and x-ray, even though provided by a physician or other licensed professional personnel.
The injury log shall contain the following information:
Company name;
Location;
Date;
Certificate number;
Type of inflatable amusement attraction;
Nature of incident;
Description of injury;
Age of injured person;
Last known condition of injured person, including date information obtained; and
Date added to log.
Each injury required under this section and §A of this regulation, shall be recorded on the injury log within 7 calendar days and updated as additional required information becomes available.
A copy of a sample injury log is available on the Department's webpage.
An owner shall maintain injury logs on a calendar year basis.
An owner shall certify the completeness and accuracy of the information contained on the injury log by January 15 of the following calendar year.
An owner shall provide access to prior years', and the portion completed for the current year, injury log to the Commissioner.
Each owner shall maintain, and make available for inspection by the Commissioner, an injury log for 5 years.
The Inflatable Amusement Attraction Daily Inspection and Daily Pre-Opening Checklist shall be:
Signed by the person performing the inspection immediately upon completion of the inspection;
Kept with the inflatable amusement attraction or at an on-site office; and
Maintained for at least 30 days.
Identification Records. An owner shall maintain a record of the name and identification number of each operator and attendant with the following information:
Inflatable amusement attraction that the operator and attendant operated; and
The date and location of operation.
Record of Training.
Each owner shall:
Maintain a record of training provided to each operator and attendant; and
Provide the Commissioner with a copy of the record of training on request.
The training record shall indicate the following for each operator and attendant:
The name or identification number;
A detailed description of the training provided; and
A list of all the inflatable amusement attractions for which the operator and attendant received training.
Written Logs.
An owner shall ensure that a written log is maintained and:
Written log requirements:
Contain any reports from riders complaining of adverse physical effects or operating inconsistencies occurring during the operation of an amusement attraction; and
Are submitted to the Commissioner when multiple reports of a particular nature may indicate a pattern are shown in the log; and

Contains weather reports that reflect review of the weather forecast prior to each set up of the inflatable amusement attraction including the date, time, location of operation, and description of weather conditions.
A copy of the written logs required by this section shall be made available to the Commissioner upon request.
An owner shall ensure that an inflatable amusement attraction is designed and manufactured in accordance with Regulation .09 of this chapter.
Upon request, an owner shall provide the Commissioner with information to document compliance with §A of this regulation.
Copies of all documents provided to the Commissioner shall be in English.
Access to and egress from all inflatable amusement attractions shall be free from all recognized hazards.
All walking surfaces including, but not limited to, loading and unloading areas, platforms, landings, stairs, and ramps shall be stable and slip-resistant.
If there is the potential for exposure to a fall distance exceeding 30 inches, the owner shall take steps to prevent such falls.

section.heading: Signage.
section.text: An owner shall comply with the signage requirements of ASTM F2374-22.7.5.4 excluding “such as, but not limited to, those listed in 7.9” in Section 7.5.4.1.

para.text: Weather-Related and Environmental Condition Restrictions.
Except as provided in §A(2) of this regulation, all manufacturer weather-related and environmental condition restrictions shall be complied with; and.

In the event of lightning, maximum sustained wind speed in excess of 15 mph, or wind gusts lasting 3 seconds or longer with wind speed of 25 mph or greater in the immediate area where the inflatable amusement attraction is operating, a tornado watch, a severe storm predicted within a 5 mile radius, fire, or other disturbance, an owner shall implement the following:
Users shall be unloaded or evacuated safely from the inflatable amusement attraction immediately; and
The inflatable amusement attraction shall be closed and secured immediately.
Illumination.
Each inflatable amusement attraction, including entrances and exits, shall be illuminated to provide adequate visibility of the surfaces used.
For the assembly and disassembly of inflatable amusement attractions, all work areas shall be illuminated to provide adequate visibility.
Flammable and Combustible Liquids.
An owner shall store and handle flammable and combustible liquids consistent with the applicable National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) standards.
An owner may not handle or fuel equipment with flammable liquids within 20 feet of an inflatable amusement attraction.
An owner shall store flammable and combustible liquids at least 75 feet from an inflatable amusement attraction or other spark-producing equipment.
Fire Extinguishers.

An owner shall provide at least one portable fire extinguisher listed and labeled with a ABC rating, as defined by the National Electrical Code, ANSI/NFPA #70, of not less than 10 pound capacity rating.
The fire extinguisher shall be located not less than 25 feet, or more than 75 feet, from any:
Flammable or combustible liquid storage; or
Portable electric generator.
Guarding.
The owner shall ensure that machinery is guarded to prevent contact with belts, fan blades, or similar moving components.
Guards shall be:
Designed to perform a specific task;
Properly secured; and
Properly maintained in a condition consistent with the intent of the guard’s design.
Before a guard is removed for maintenance purposes:
The equipment shall be locked out or tagged out; and
The guard shall be replaced before normal operation is resumed.
A guard shall be designed so that it does not pose a hazard itself.
Portable Electric Generators.
A portable electric generator is not required to be grounded if the portable electric generator:
Supplies only equipment mounted on the generator; or
Supplies power to the equipment through receptacles mounted on the generator that are connected by plug and cord and single extensions.
If a portable electric generator does not comply with §B(1) of this regulation, the owner shall ensure that a grounding electrode, such as a ground rod, is used for any fault current.
A portable electric generator shall be listed and labeled by a Nationally Recognized Testing Laboratory.
A portable electric generator may not be located:
Near user or pedestrian traffic; or
In locations that may be hazardous.
In addition to the requirements set forth in this section, an owner shall comply with the installation requirements set forth in ASTM F2374.22.7.5.6.2-7.5.6.10.
Location.
Placement of the inflatable amusement attraction at the location where it is to be operated shall ensure that:
The inflatable amusement attraction is compliant with manufacturer specifications;
The inflatable amusement attraction is on a level and stable surface after consideration of slope, topography and surface type;
The inflatable amusement attraction is not placed in a public right of way and away from motor vehicle traffic; and
The inflatable amusement attraction is accessible by emergency personnel.
Tie-Downs.
Tie-downs and anchors used to support an inflatable amusement attraction may not create a tripping or impalement hazard.
The immediate area surrounding the inflatable amusement attraction shall be clear of:
Trees and shrubs, if they pose a hazard;
Free from debris; and
Other tripping hazards including, but not limited to, pot holes.
Clearances.

Except for equipment required for operation of the inflatable amusement attraction, there shall be side and overhead clearances of at least 48 inches between an inflatable amusement attraction and any other object.
Except for conductors supplying power to the device, an inflatable amusement attraction may not operate under or within 15 feet of any power lines, utilities, roads, cliffs, or bodies of water.
In addition to the requirements set forth in this section, an owner shall comply with the installation requirements set forth in ASTM F2374-22.5.16.4.
Lighting systems. Lighting systems, including fluorescent lighting systems, shall be:
In good repair;
Protected against accidental contact and breakage; and
Capable of containing the pieces of a broken light should breakage occur.

section.heading: Rules of Procedure for Hearings.
section.text: All contested case hearings before the Commissioner shall be governed by COMAR 09.01.02.

para.text: Application for Variance.
An affected owner may apply to the Commissioner for a variance from any regulation adopted under Business Regulation Article, Title 3, Annotated Code of Maryland.
An application for variance shall be in writing and shall include:
The name and address of the applicant;
Identification of the regulation from which the applicant seeks a variance;
The location, equipment, and practices involved;
A detailed statement of the applicant’s reasons for requesting a variance;
A statement of the steps the applicant has taken and will take, with specific dates when appropriate, to protect the public against the specific hazard covered by the regulation; and
Any request for a hearing.
Posting of Notice.

Immediately on filing a request for a variance pursuant to Business Regulation Article, §3-314, Annotated Code of Maryland, an applicant shall post notice of the application at the place or places where a certificate of inspection is required to be posted.
The notice shall include:
The name and address of the applicant;
The date on which the application was filed;
Identification of the regulation from which the applicant seeks a variance;
The location, equipment, and practices involved; and
Notice to the public and affected employees of their right to participate in the proceeding.
The notice shall be maintained until the commencement of the proceedings.
Order of Variance.
After investigation, on-site inspection, and such hearing as the Commissioner may direct, the Commissioner may grant a variance from a regulation under this chapter if:
It is evident that action is necessary to prevent undue hardship; or
Existing conditions prevent practical compliance and the reasonable safety of the public can, in the opinion of the Commissioner, be ensured.
An order of variance may require such special conditions as are reasonably necessary to ensure public safety.

A declaration, act, or omission of the Commissioner or the Commissioner’s authorized representative, other than a written order authorizing a variance as permitted above, may not be construed to exempt, either wholly or in part, expressly or implicitly, any owner from full compliance with the terms of any regulation under this chapter.
Modification, Revocation, and Renewal of Order of Variance.
The Commissioner may modify, revoke, or renew an order of variance in the same manner prescribed for the issuance of an order of variance.
An order of variance may not be modified or revoked unless affected parties are informed of the proposed action and afforded an opportunity to:
Submit written data, views, or arguments regarding the proposed action; and
Request a hearing on the proposal.
