para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Activities of daily living” means functions normally associated with mobility, eating, elimination, body hygiene and dressing.
“Attending” means the role of an individual who establishes the plan of treatment and certifies the necessity for home health services for a participant following a hospitalization.
“Case coordinator” means a licensed health professional designated by a home health agency to coordinate the care of a participant.
“Certified nurse midwife (CNM)” means an individual who meets licensure requirements and conditions for participation as a certified nurse midwife set forth in COMAR 10.09.01.
“Certified nurse practitioner” means an individual who meets the licensing requirements and conditions for participation as a nurse practitioner set forth in COMAR 10.09.01.

“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health, the single State agency designated to administer the Maryland Medical Assistance Program under Title XIX of the Social Security Act, 42 U.S.C. 1396 et seq.
“Electronic Visit Verification (EVV)” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.36.03-2.
“Face-to-face” means contact with a participant that occurs in person or via audio-visual telehealth in accordance with COMAR 10.09.49.
“Health team” means the physician or nonphysician practitioner and the home health agency personnel who render services listed in Regulation .04B of this chapter to a participant.
“Home” means the place of residence occupied by the participant, including a domiciliary level facility, but other than a hospital, nursing facility, or other medical institution.
“Home health agency” means a public or private agency or organization, or part of an agency or organization, that meets the requirements for participation in Medicare.
“Home health aide” means a person who meets the requirements of COMAR 10.07.10, and has received instruction from, and renders services under the supervision of, a registered nurse.
“In person” means contact with a participant that occurs in the physical presence of the participant and the provider.
“Intermittent” means services which are furnished on a medically predictable recurring basis.
“Licensed nurse” means a person who is licensed as a registered nurse or as a licensed practical nurse in the jurisdiction in which the service is provided.
“Medicaid average rate” means the average projected per diem rate established for the Department's fiscal year as specified in COMAR 10.09.10.07B(2) multiplied by 30.
“Medically necessary” means that the service or benefit is:
Directly related to diagnostic, preventive, curative, palliative, rehabilitative, or ameliorative treatment of an illness, injury, disability, or health condition;
Consistent with current accepted standards of good medical practice;
The most cost efficient service that can be provided without sacrificing effectiveness or access to care; and
Not primarily for the convenience of the consumer, family, or provider.

“Medical supplies” means items which are generally recognized under accepted standards of medical practice as serving a therapeutic or diagnostic purpose and are medically necessary to enable home health agency personnel to carry out effectively the care the physician has ordered for the treatment or diagnosis of the patient's illness or injury, and which are accounted for in billing records.
“Medicare” means the insurance program administered by the federal government under Title XVIII of the Social Security Act, 42 U.S.C. 1395 et seq.
“Newborn early discharge assessment” means a visit to the newborn infant and postpartum mother to observe and assess the health status of both the newborn and the mother.
“Nonphysician practitioner” means an individual who:
Is licensed as a certified nurse practitioner, clinical nurse specialist, a certified nurse-midwife, or a physician assistant in the jurisdiction in which the service is provided; and
Works in collaboration with or under the supervision of the ordering practitioner.
“Occupational therapist” means a person who is licensed or registered as an occupational therapist in the jurisdiction in which the service is provided.
“Participant” means a person who is certified as eligible for and is receiving Medical Assistance benefits.

“Part-time” means services, usually furnished during several visits per week, but not exceeding one visit per discipline per day, unless unusual circumstances are documented by the physician, which are reasonable and necessary to the treatment of an illness or injury.
“Physical therapist” means a person who is licensed or registered as a physical therapist in the jurisdiction in which the service is provided.
“Physician” means an individual who meets the licensure requirements and conditions for participation set forth in COMAR 10.09.02.
“Physician assistant” means an individual who meets license requirements and conditions for participation set forth in COMAR 10.09.55.
“Program” means the Maryland Medical Assistance Program.

“Progress note” means a dated, written notation by a member of the health team which summarizes facts about the care given and the patient's response during a given period of time, specifically addresses the established goals of treatment, is consistent with the patient plan of care, is written immediately following each visit, and is part of the provider's permanent record for the participant.

“Provider” means a person or an organization who meets the requirements of Regulations .03 and .04 of this chapter and who, through an appropriate agreement with the Department, has been identified as a Program provider by the issuance of an individual account number.
“Speech-language pathologist” means a person who is licensed as a speech-language pathologist in the jurisdiction in which the service is provided.

“Supervision” means authoritative procedural guidance by a qualified person for the accomplishment of a function or activity with initial direction and periodic evaluation of the act of accomplishing the function or activity.
“Support system” means a family member, friend, neighbor, or any person who renders services, which would otherwise be covered under Regulation .04 of this chapter, to the participant.
“Telehealth” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.49.02.
“Visit” means the time spent rendering a covered service to a participant at home by an individual employed by a home health agency.
“Witness” means a person who on behalf of the participant is able to personally verify at the time of service that the participant received home health care.
The provider shall submit his request for payment on the form designated by the Department.

To receive payment for services under Regulation .04 of this chapter, a provider and its workers shall use the Electronic Visit Verification method and data management system approved by the Department to document time and submit claims in accordance with COMAR 10.09.36.03-2.
The provider shall submit requests for payment for supplies on the same invoice which requests payment for the visit during which the supplies were used.
The Department reserves the right to return to the provider, before payment, all invoices not properly completed.
Payment Rates.
The provider shall be paid the lesser of:
The provider's customary charge to the general public unless the service is free to individuals not covered by Medicaid; or
The rate in accordance with the Department's fee schedule.
For out-of-State providers, rates shall be paid at the lower of the following:
The home state's Medicaid rate for the same service rendered by the same provider if the provider participates in its home state Medicaid Program; or
The rate paid for the same service rendered by a provider in the nearest Maryland county.

The Department shall pay home health providers for medical and other supplies which are used during a covered home health visit as part of the treatment ordered by the participant's physician, physician assistant, certified nurse midwife, or certified nurse practitioner at a rate that is the lesser of the:
Provider's customary charge to the general public unless the service is free to individuals not covered by Medicaid; or
Medicaid rate for the supply or pharmaceutical under COMAR 10.09.12 and 10.09.03.

Subject to the limitations of the State budget, the fee schedule rates shall be adjusted annually by the same factor used by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services in updating Medicare's prospective payment system rates. The annual fee schedule rate adjustment shall be limited to a maximum of 5 percent and be effective the date on which Medicare's rate changes are implemented.
The fee schedule effective January 1, 2025 is as follows:
Billing time limitations for claims submitted pursuant to this chapter are set forth in COMAR 10.09.36.
The Department shall reimburse for supervision of home health aide services by a registered nurse as part of the home health aide reimbursement.
Payment to a provider of newborn early discharge services may not exceed the rate for one skilled nursing visit.

The Department shall reimburse for preauthorized home health services upon verification that a face-to-face encounter with the participant was performed as described under Regulation .03C of this chapter.
The home health provider shall identify the individual who ordered the home health services by recording the individual practitioner's National Provider Identifier (NPI) number on the claim.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Administrative service organization (ASO)” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.59.01.
“ASO system” means the electronic system available to providers which the ASO utilizes to manage Maryland’s Public Behavioral Health System as defined in COMAR 10.09.59.01.

“Care management tool” means a system that helps accomplish administrative tasks of the health home, including maintaining a list of health home participants and scheduling and tracking participants clinical appointments.

“Care plan” means a written plan of action that is developed and modified to address a patient's specific behavioral, somatic, and social service needs, which is maintained in the individual's medical record and satisfies the following conditions:
Meets the requirements of COMAR 10.47.01.04C; or
Meets the requirements of COMAR 10.21.21.06C.
“Caregiver” means a family member, guardian, or other individual who is not paid to provide care to the participant and who helps the participant achieve and maintain wellness.

“Chesapeake Regional Information System for Our Patients (CRISP) means the electronic notification system health home providers are required to use in order to access participant hospital encounter data.

“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health, the State agency designated to administer the Maryland Medical Assistance Program under Title XIX of the Social Security Act, 42 U.S.C §1396 et seq.
“Full-time equivalent (FTE)” means an employee who works 40 hours per week.

“Health home” means a provider designated to offer enhanced care coordination and management services to individuals affected by, or at risk for, chronic conditions, operating under the conditions of this chapter.

“Health Information Technology” means electronic systems equipped for the exchange of health data to link services, facilitate communication between health team members and individuals and family caregivers, and enable quality reporting and the review of health outcomes.
“Managed care organization (MCO)” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §15-101, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Medical Assistance Program” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.36.01.

“Mental health case management” means services covered under COMAR 10.09.45 and COMAR 10.09.90 which assist participants in gaining access to the full range of mental health services, and necessary medical, social, financial assistance, counseling, educational, housing, and other support services.
“Minor” means an individual who is younger than 18 years old.
“Mobile treatment services (MTS) program” means a program approved under COMAR 10.63.03.04.
“Opioid treatment program (OTP)”, formerly referred to as opioid maintenance therapy (OMT) programs, means a program approved to provide opioid maintenance therapy under COMAR 10.63.03.19.
“Participant” means an individual enrolled in a health home funded by the Medical Assistance Program.
“Provider” means an individual, association, partnership, corporation, unincorporated group, or any other person authorized, licensed, or certified to provide health services.
“Psychiatric rehabilitation program (PRP)” means a program approved under COMAR 10.63.03.09 for adults, COMAR 10.63.03.10 for minors, or both.

section.heading: Conditions for Health Home Provider Participation.
section.text: To be eligible as a health home, a provider shall:

para.text: Meet the conditions for provider participation in the Medical Assistance Program, as set forth in COMAR 10.09.36.03;
Meet the approval requirements set forth in Regulation .02 of this chapter;
Be accredited by, or demonstrate evidence of having started the accreditation process from, an approved accrediting body as a health home;

For PRP and MTS health homes serving minors, demonstrate a minimum of 3 years of experience serving minors, which may be achieved as an independent practice or as a member of a broader agency, with exceptions designated by the Department;
At the time of enrollment as a health home, be registered or be able to provide documentation of starting the process of registration with CRISP in order to receive hospital encounter alerts;

At the time of enrollment as a health home, be registered or be able to provide documentation of starting the process of registration with one of the following organizations in order to receive access to real-time pharmacy data for participants:
CRISP; or
The State’s ASO;
At the time of enrollment as a health home, have an internal protocol for reviewing and responding to hospital encounter alerts and pharmacy use data;
Directly provide, or subcontract for the provision of, health home services to all participants;
Maintain an electronic database with the ability to, at minimum:
Maintain an up-to-date list of all health home participants and their contact information; and
Record and review clinical appointments;
Maintain a file for each participant that includes:
A form signed by the participant consenting to participate in the health home, including the program’s data-sharing elements;
An initial assessment of the participant’s health and social services needs, as described in Regulation .06B(1)(a) of this chapter; and
A care plan, updated every 6 months, which may be combined with the existing MTS, PRP, or OTP care plan, and includes, at a minimum:
The participant’s health home goals;
Time frames for meeting the health home goals;
Proposed interventions for meeting the health home goals;
Relevant community networks and supports;
Optimal clinical outcomes for the participant; and
Signatures of:
The participant or the participant’s parent or guardian; and
The nurse care manager to whom the participant has been assigned in the health home;
Safeguard the confidentiality of the participants’ records in accordance with State and federal laws and regulations;

Provide on-call and crisis intervention services by telephone 24 hours a day, 7 days a week to participants and, as appropriate, their caregivers, or if the participant is a minor, the minor’s parent or guardian;
Be responsible for meeting all health home service requirements, including services performed by a business or individual subcontracted to provide such services;
Convene health home staff meetings every 6 months, at minimum, to plan and implement goals and objectives of functioning as a health home;
Collaborate with MCOs and the ASO to improve participant outcomes; and
Agree to participate in federal and State-required evaluation activities, including:
Using the ASO system to:
Submit encounters documenting participants’ services at least monthly;
Generate monthly reports documenting:
Health home service delivery; and
Participants’ health and social outcomes; and
Update participant diagnoses and outcomes every 6 months; and
Completing and submitting to the Department a program assessment every 6 months to demonstrate that:
All staffing and other regulatory requirements are being met; and
A quality improvement plan is being implemented.
The Department covers the services in §§B—G of this regulation when these services have been documented, pursuant to the requirements in this chapter, as necessary.
Comprehensive Care Management. The health home shall collaborate to provide comprehensive care management services including:
An initial assessment performed prior to the patient's enrollment, which includes:

A comprehensive assessment of the participant's physical health, mental health, chemical dependency, and social service needs, signed off on by a physician or nurse practitioner, if no such assessment has been performed in the preceding 6-month period; and
Requesting records from the participant's primary care physician and other providers;
Development of a care plan within 30 days following enrollment, in accordance with Regulation .04J(3) of this chapter;
Delineation of roles, which includes:
Assigning each staff member clear roles and responsibilities; and
Ensuring that participant care plans identify providers and specialists involved in the participant's care; and
Monitoring and reassessment, which includes:
Monitoring and documenting participant health status and progress toward care plan goals;
Monitoring population health status and service use to determine adherence to or variance from treatment guidelines; and
Outcomes evaluation and reporting, which includes using eMedicaid and other available HIT tools such as electronic health records.
Care Coordination and Health Promotion.
The health home shall coordinate and provide access to:
High-quality health care services;
Preventive and health promotion services, including education regarding:
Mental illness;
Substance use disorders; and
Chronic physical health conditions;
Mental health and substance abuse services;
Chronic disease management services; and
Long-term care supports and services.
The health home shall coordinate services and support, including:
Appointment scheduling;
Referrals and follow-up monitoring;
Hospital discharge processes; and
Communication with other providers and supports, including school service providers.
The health home shall assign each participant a health home care manager who is responsible for coordinating the participant's care and ensuring implementation of the care plan.

The health home shall develop policies and procedures to facilitate collaboration between primary care, specialist, and behavioral health providers, community-based organizations, and, for minors, school-based providers.
The health home shall follow security protocols to protect confidential health information.
The health home shall assist participants with the implementation of their care plan, including:
Health education specific to a participant's chronic conditions;
Development of a plan for self-management;
Medication review and education; and
Substance use prevention, smoking cessation, obesity reduction, improved nutrition, and increased physical activity.
A health home serving minors shall actively involve parents and families in providing services in accordance with §C(6) of this regulation, including:
Identifying conditions for which the minor may be at risk due to family, physical, or social factors; and
Working with the minor and parents and families to address the identified conditions.
The health home shall use the ASO system to document, review, and report health promotion services delivered to each participant.
Comprehensive Transitional Care.
The health home shall provide services designed to:
Streamline plans of care;
Reduce avoidable hospital admissions;
Ease the transition to long-term services;
Interrupt patterns of frequent hospital emergency department use; and
Ensure timely and proper follow-up care across settings, including from:
An acute care setting to other settings; and
A pediatric system of care to an adult system of care.
The health home shall increase participants' and caregivers' ability to manage care and live safely in the community.
The health home shall utilize CRISP to receive alerts of hospital admissions, discharges, or transfers among their health home participants.
The health home shall follow up with participants within 2 business days of discharge with a home visit, phone call, or scheduling an on-site appointment.
Individual and Family Support Services.
Services shall include, but are not limited to:
Advocating for individuals and families;
Supporting participants in obtaining and adhering to medications and other prescribed treatments;
Accessing resources that support participants, including providing referrals for:
Community services;
Social support services;
Recovery services; and
Transportation to medically necessary services;
Improving participants' health literacy;
Increasing the participant's ability to self-manage care;
Facilitating participation in the ongoing revision of the treatment plan; and
Providing information on advance directives and health care power of attorney.
The health home shall utilize peer supports, support groups, and self-care programs to:
Increase participants' and caregivers' knowledge of the participants' diseases;
Increase caregivers' care-management capabilities;
Promote participants' adherence to their plan of care; and
Increase participants' self-management capabilities.
The health home shall ensure that all communication shared with the participant, the participant's family, and caregivers is language, literacy, and culturally appropriate.
Referral to Community and Social Support Services. The health home shall provide assistance in accessing and coordinating, as appropriate:
Medical assistance;
Disability benefits;
Subsidized or supported housing;
Personal needs support;
Peer support; or
Legal services.
The health home shall assist in coordinating these services.
Use of HIT to Link Services. The provider shall use HIT, including CRISP and the ASO system to:
Facilitate communication between health home staff members, the participant, and their caregivers; and
As appropriate, provide feedback to participants' other providers.
Health home services provided by PRP, MTS, or OTP staff qualify as covered services.
Enrollment.
The health home shall enroll an individual only after the individual has been enrolled in the health home provider's applicable PRP, MTS, or OTP services.

During enrollment, an OTP established as a health home shall identify eligible individuals under the OTP’s care and report the diagnoses related to qualifying risk factors set forth in Regulation .03A(2)(b) of this chapter in the ASO system.
The health home shall provide the individual with a brief description of health home services, including:
Explaining the data-sharing elements of the program; and
Describing how the individual may opt out if desired.
Following the provision of information in accordance with §A(3) of this regulation, the health home shall obtain the individual's consent to participate in the health home.

Following consent to participate in accordance with §A(4) of this regulation, the health home shall complete the individual's online intake report through the ASO system thereby enrolling the individual into the health home.
The health home shall:
Notify a participant's other treatment providers about health home services; and
Encourage other providers' participation in care coordination efforts.
Participation.
A health home participant shall receive a minimum of two health home services per month, as defined in Regulation .06 of this chapter and to be documented in the ASO system.
An assigned health home care manager shall monitor the participant's care and health status and coordinate with other staff to provide appropriate health home services.
Discharge.
In the event of discharge, the health home shall create a discharge plan that includes referrals to appropriate services and providers.
The health home shall report all discharges and completion of discharge plans in the ASO system.
An eligible individual may not receive services from a health home provider that is not the individual’s PRP, MTS, or OTP provider.
Health home services do not restrict or otherwise affect:
Eligibility for Title XIX benefits or other available benefits or programs, except as limited by §E of this regulation;
The freedom of a participant to select from all available services for which the participant is found to be eligible; or
A participant’s free choice among providers in the Medical Assistance Program.
The Department may not reimburse the health home provider for:
Activities that have already been billed to or counted towards a service requirement for another Medical Assistance Program or other program;
Activities not consistent with the definition of health home services under this chapter;
Activities delivered as part of institutional discharge planning that are not comprehensive transitional care services delivered by the health home;
A participant’s health home monthly rate more than once per month;
A participant’s health home monthly rate when the intake encounter is utilized by the provider as one of the two required monthly service encounters;
A participant’s health home monthly rate when the provider counts two encounters as having the same date of service, unless:
The modality of the two encounters is different because one was an individual encounter and one was a group encounter; or
The encounters are distinguishable by both the time at which they were rendered and the subject matter discussed; or
The health home monthly rate when the two required services were both rendered as group services.
The Department may not reimburse for monthly health home services unless the individual receiving health home services:
Is Medicaid eligible at the time of service delivery and engaged in treatment or rehabilitation with either OTP or PRP or MTS services;
Is enrolled as a health home member at the billing health home provider; and
Has received a minimum of two health home services in the stated month that has been documented in the ASO system.
Reimbursement will not be made for health home services if the participant is receiving a comparable service under another Medical Assistance Program or other program.
A participant’s health home provider may not be the participant’s family member.
The Department shall reimburse the health home for covered services according to the requirements in this chapter and the rate established in §C of this regulation.
Request for Payment.
The health home provider is authorized to bill for the intake and ongoing monthly rate for a participant when:
The participant is receiving PRP, MTS, or OTP services; and
The intake portion of the participant's ASO system file has been submitted and initial services have been delivered.

After completing the required health home service provision reporting in the ASO system, the health home provider shall, within 30 days from the end of the month during which health home services were provided, submit a request for payment for all participants who received two health home services during that month.
A health home provider shall bill the Department for the appropriate rate specified in §C of this regulation.
The Department shall reimburse according to the following fee schedule:
For dates of service from July 1, 2023 through December 31, 2023, at a monthly rate of $131.03 per participant;
For dates of service from January 1, 2024 through June 30, 2024, at a monthly rate of $141.51; and
For dates of service beginning July 1, 2024, at a monthly rate of $145.76.

para.text: Payment procedures are as set forth in COMAR 10.09.36.04 and .06.
Payments.
Payments shall be made directly to a qualified provider.

To receive payment for services under Regulation .04 of this chapter, a provider and its workers shall use the electronic visit verification method and data management system approved by the Department to document time and submit claims in accordance with COMAR 10.09.36.03-2.
Providers shall be paid the lesser of:
The provider’s customary charge to the general public unless the service is free to individuals not covered by Medicaid; or
The rate established according to the fee schedule published by the Department.
Rates.
Effective July 1, 2022, through December 31, 2023, rates for the services outlined in this regulation shall be as follows:
$17.87 for 15 minutes of services provided by a registered nurse to one participant;
$12.32 for 15 minutes of services provided by a registered nurse to each of two or more participants in the same residence;
$11.58 for 15 minutes of services provided by a licensed practical nurse to one participant;
$7.99 for 15 minutes of services provided by a licensed practical nurse to each of two or more participants in the same residence;
$6.12 for 15 minutes of services provided by a CNA or HHA who is also certified as a CMT to one participant;
$4.22 for 15 minutes of services provided by a CNA or HHA who is also certified as a CMT to each of two or more participants in the same residence;
$5.08 for 15 minutes of services provided by a CNA or HHA to one participant;
$3.50 for 15 minutes of services provided by a CNA or HHA to two or more participants in the same residence; and
A flat rate of $71.41 per visit for a registered nurse supervisory visit of a nurse, CNA, or HHA.
Effective January 1, 2024 through June 30, 2024, rates for the services outlined in this regulation shall be as follows:
$19.2951 for 15 minutes of services provided by a registered nurse to one participant;

$12.5061 for 15 minutes of services provided by a licensed practical nurse to one participant;
$8.6266 for 15 minutes of services provided by a licensed practical nurse to each of two or more participants in the same residence;
$6.6105 for 15 minutes of services provided by a CNA or HHA who is also certified as a CMT to one participant;
$4.5611 for 15 minutes of services provided by a CNA or HHA who is also certified as a CMT to each of two or more participants in the same residence;
$5.4874 for 15 minutes of services provided by a CNA or HHA to one participant;
$3.7848 for 15 minutes of services provided by a CNA or HHA to two or more participants in the same residence; and
A flat rate of $77.13 per visit for a registered nurse supervisory visit of a nurse, CNA, or HHA.
Effective July 1, 2024, rates for the services outlined in this regulation shall be as follows:
$19.8739 for 15 minutes of services provided by a registered nurse to one participant;
$13.7094 for 15 minutes of services provided by a registered nurse to each of two or more participants in the same residence;

A flat rate of $79.44 per visit for a registered nurse supervisory visit of a nurse, CNA, or HHA.
The Program’s rates as specified in this regulation shall adjust each year, subject to the limitations of the State budget.
The nursing services provider shall identify the individual who ordered the nursing services by recording the individual practitioner’s National Provider Identifier (NPI) number on the claim.

para.text: General Requirements.
To be eligible for participation, an individual shall be determined by the Department to meet the conditions of §§B—E of this regulation.
Eligibility for waiver services shall be reevaluated every 12 months or more frequently if needed due to a significant change in the participant’s condition, needs, or financial status.

Technical Eligibility. To be eligible for services covered under this chapter, an applicant or participant shall be determined by the Department to meet the technical eligibility criteria if the individual:
Has been determined by the Department to need a nursing facility level of care;
Is at least 18 years old;
Is not simultaneously enrolled for services covered under:
Another Medicaid waiver program under the authority of §1915(c) of the Social Security Act; or
The Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE);
Has an active plan of service that:
Is based on:
The assessment and recommended plan of care; and
Consultation with the applicant or participant;
Addresses the applicant’s or participant’s needs;
Specifies the names of service providers;

Is cost neutral, which is determined by adding annualized costs of services covered under this chapter and any other State Plan services which are not covered for nursing facility residents, and ensuring that the resulting amount is not more than 125 percent of the Program's average per capita-annualized-net payments for nursing facility services. Any assessed participant contributions will not be considered in determining cost neutrality; and
Includes the signature of the participant, the individual’s representative if applicable, and the case manager listed within the plan of service;
Is offered the choice between waiver services and nursing facility services;
Chooses to receive waiver services;
Resides in a home, as defined under Regulation .02B of this chapter; and
Uses at least one waiver service within a 12-month period.
Medical Assistance Eligibility.
An individual is not eligible to receive waiver services during a penalty period imposed under COMAR 10.09.24.08-1 or 10.09.24.08-2 due to disposal of assets.
All provisions of COMAR 10.09.24 which are applicable to aged, blind, or disabled institutionalized persons are applicable to waiver applicants and participants, with the following exceptions:
Cost of Care.

For a participant whose home is an assisted living facility, the Department shall reduce its monthly payment for assisted living services by the amount remaining after deducting from the individual's total nonexcluded monthly income the following amounts in the following order:

A personal needs allowance, consisting of the amount established in accordance with COMAR 10.09.24.10D(2)(c) and the assisted living provider's charge, not exceeding $420 per month, for room and board;
A spousal or family maintenance allowance, or both, if applicable, in accordance with COMAR 10.09.24.10-1C(3)(b) and (c); and
Incurred medical expenses in accordance with COMAR 10.09.24.10D(2)(f)—(h).
The Department shall determine the amount of available income to be paid by a participant towards the cost of assisted living services.

The participant shall pay the amount of available income for the participant’s cost of care, and the assisted living provider’s monthly charge for room and board, directly to the assisted living services provider.

Waiver Eligibility. Based on the criteria established in §§A—C of this regulation an applicant's eligibility for services under this chapter shall be established by the Department based on the following policies for the effective date of waiver eligibility:
No retroactive eligibility; and
Waiver eligibility may not begin before the latest of the following five dates:
Waiver application date;
Effective date of medical certification for the waiver's institutional level of care;

Date that the applicant's written waiver plan of service is established, which shall include at least one waiver service and may be a provisional plan for not more than the first 60 days of waiver enrollment;
Date that the applicant or representative signed a form designated by the Department to indicate the choice of waiver services as an alternative to institutionalization; and
Date of the applicant's discharge from institutionalization in a long term care facility, if applicable.
Annual Cap and Registry for Waiver Participation.

The Department shall establish an annual cap, approved by the federal Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS), for the number of unduplicated individuals who may receive the services covered under this chapter, based on available State and federal funding.
Eligible individuals shall be enrolled in the waiver on a first-come, first-served basis until the annual cap on waiver participation is reached.
Once the annual cap on waiver participation is reached:
A registry list shall be established for individuals interested in applying for waiver services;
Individuals on the registry shall have an opportunity to apply for the waiver in accordance with procedures established by the Department; and

The Department and CMS may authorize increasing the waiver cap if the Department determines that sufficient Program funds are available to reimburse the services recommended in the individual's plan of service and the participant's other Program services for the remainder of the State fiscal year.
Individuals in nursing facilities who are receiving Medicaid services for at least 30 days may apply directly for the waiver without being put on the registry list.
Termination of Participation.
A participant shall be terminated from participation in the waiver if the participant:
No longer meets the eligibility requirements specified in §§B—E of this regulation;
Voluntarily chooses, or the participant’s authorized representative chooses on the participant’s behalf, to disenroll from the waiver program;
Moves to another state;
Is an inpatient for 30 consecutive days or more in a hospital or nursing facility; or
Dies.

If an individual is terminated from the waiver, the same individual may re-enter the waiver during the same waiver year, or within 90 days of termination contingent on waiver capacity, provided that the individual meets all of the eligibility requirements of the waiver.

section.heading: Conditions for Provider Participation — General Requirements.

para.text: To participate as a provider of a service covered under this chapter a provider shall:
Meet all of the conditions for participation as a Maryland Medical Assistance Program provider as set forth in COMAR 10.09.36, except as otherwise specified in this chapter;
Verify the qualifications of all individuals who render services on the provider's behalf, and provide a copy of the current license or credentials upon request;
Implement the reporting and follow-up of incidents and complaints in accordance with the Department's established reportable events policy;
Agree to cooperate with required inspections, reviews, and audits by authorized governmental representatives;

Agree to provide services, and to subsequently bill the Department in accordance with the reimbursement methodology specified in this chapter for only those services covered under this chapter which have been:
Pre-approved in the participant's plan of service;
Provided in a manner consistent with the participant's plan of service; and
Identified in the provider agreement as within the scope of the provider's Medicaid participation;

Agree to maintain and have available written documentation of services, including dates and hours of services provided to participants, for a period of 6 years, in a manner approved by the Department;

Agree not to suspend, terminate, increase, or reduce services for an individual without authorization from the Department and with consultation and agreement by the participant or a participant's representative when applicable;
Submit a transition plan to the case manager and participant or participant's representative when applicable when suspending or terminating services;
Verify Medicaid eligibility at the beginning of each month that services will be rendered;
Not be a Medicaid provider or principal of a Medicaid provider that has overpayments that remain due to the Department;
Notify the Department in writing at least 45 days in advance of any:
Voluntary closure;
Change of ownership;
Change of location;
Sale of the business;
Change in the name under which the provider is doing business; or
Change in provider tax identification number;

Include in the notice to the Department regarding any change of status under §A(11) of this regulation, the method for informing waiver participants and representatives of its intent to close, change ownership, change location, or sell its business;

Apply for a new license if applicable, whenever ownership is to be transferred from the person or organization named on the license to another person or organization in time to assure continuity of waiver services;
Submit a Medicaid provider application to the Department if the new owner chooses to participate in the waiver program;

Obtain a state and federal background check through the Criminal Justice Information System on all staff in accordance with the procedure for a State criminal history records check established under Health-General Article, Title 19, Subtitle 19, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Render services in person, except as expressly authorized by the Department in the regulation governing that service type; and
If rendering services via telehealth, comply with COMAR 10.09.49 and any subregulatory guidance issued by the Department.
To participate as a provider of a service covered under this chapter, a provider or its principals may not, within the past 24 months, have:
Had a license or certificate suspended or revoked as a health care provider, health care facility, or provider of direct care services;
Undergone the imposition of sanctions under COMAR 10.09.36.08;
Been subject to disciplinary action, including actions by the licensing board, that indicate behavior which is potentially harmful to participants;
Been cited by a State agency for deficiencies which affect participants health and safety; or
Experienced a termination of a Medicaid provider agreement or been barred from work or participation by a public or private agency due to:
Failure to meet contractual obligations; or
Fraudulent billing practices.
A provider who renders health-related services to participants shall agree to:
Periodically indicate the condition of a participant in accordance with the procedures and forms designated by the Department; and
Share and discuss the documented information at the request of the participant.

A provider, its principals, or its staff may not have a history of behavior or convictions that could be harmful to participants, as evidenced through a State and federal background check conducted in accordance with §A(15) of this regulation.

To receive payment as a provider of services covered under Regulations .13—.19 of this chapter, a provider shall submit claims in accordance with procedures outlined in the Department's billing manual.
Billing time limitations for the services covered under this chapter are set forth in COMAR 10.09.36.06.
Payments.
Payments shall be made directly to a qualified provider.
A provider shall be paid the lesser of:
The provider's customary charge to the general public unless the service is free to individuals not covered by Medicaid; or
The rates established in §D of this regulation.
Rates.
For dates of service beginning July 1, 2022, through June 30, 2024, fees per unit of service shall be as follows:
Assisted Living Level II, No Medical Day Care: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $81.57 per day;
Assisted Living Level III, No Medical Day Care: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $102.94 per day;
Assisted Living Level II, Medical Day Care: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $61.21 per day;
Assisted Living Level III, Medical Day Care: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $77.18 per day;
Behavioral Consultation: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $89.22 per hour;
Case Management — Administrative: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $83.66 per hour or $20.9138 per 15-minute unit;
Case Management — Comprehensive: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $83.66 per hour or $20.9138 per 15-minute unit;
Case Management — Ongoing: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $83.66 per hour or $20.9138 per 15-minute unit;
Dietitian and Nutritionist Services: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $89.22 per hour;
Family 1/1 Training: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $89.22 per hour;
Medical Day Care Services: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $104.81 per day; and
Senior Center Plus: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $64.90 per day.
For dates of service beginning July 1, 2024, fees per unit of service shall be as follows:
Assisted Living Level II, No Medical Day Care: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $90.74 per day;
Assisted Living Level III, No Medical Day Care: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $114.51 per day;
Assisted Living Level II, Medical Day Care: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $68.09 per day;
Assisted Living Level III, Medical Day Care: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $85.86 per day;
Behavioral Consultation: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $99.25 per hour;
Case Management — Administrative: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $93.06 per hour or $23.2645 per 15-minute unit;
Case Management — Comprehensive: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $93.06 per hour or $23.2645 per 15-minute unit;
Case Management — Ongoing: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $93.06 per hour or $23.2645 per 15-minute unit;
Dietitian and Nutritionist Services: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $99.25 per hour;
Family 1/1 Training: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $99.25 per hour;
Medical Day Care Services: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $116.59 per day; and
Senior Center Plus: reimbursed at the maximum rate of $72.19 per day.
The Program’s rates for covered services under Regulations .13—.19 of this chapter shall be adjusted subject to the limitations of the State budget.

para.text: Defines:
Mifepristone data;
Health information exchange;
Electronic health network; and
Protected Health Care Commission;
Lists the:
Mifepristone data, and diagnosis, procedure, and related codes for abortion care that are subject to the restrictions on the disclosure of health care information; and
Diagnosis, procedure, and related codes for gender-affirming treatment that are subject to the restrictions on the disclosure of health care information;
Describes to whom these restrictions apply; and
Sets forth the restrictions on data.
In this chapter, the following terms have the meaning indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Electronic health network” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.25.09.02.
“Gender-affirming treatment” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §15-151, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Health information exchange” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.25.18.02.

“Mifepristone data” means all medication or related codes pertaining to mifepristone use during any stage of pregnancy, and excludes any codes pertaining to the use of mifepristone for Cushing’s syndrome.
“Protected Health Care Commission” means the group established under Health-General Article, §4-310, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Secretary” means the Secretary of the Maryland Department of Health.

section.heading: Mifepristone Data Codes.
section.text: A health information exchange and an electronic health network shall restrict the disclosure of mifepristone data Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) codes and medication codes listed in the tables below.

para.text: List of Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System ( HCPCS) Level 2 Codes.

tr.td: Medically induced abortion by oral ingestion of medication including all associated services and supplies (e.g., patient counseling, office visits, confirmation of pregnancy by human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG), ultrasound to confirm duration of pregnancy, ultrasound to confirm completion of abortion) except drug/medications

para.text: List of Medication Codes.

tr.td: Mifeprex, alternate pack configuration with multiple cartons

para.text: A health information exchange and an electronic health network shall restrict the disclosure of the gender-affirming treatment medical diagnosis codes and current procedural terminology (CPT) codes listed in §B below.
List of Medical Diagnosis Codes.

tr.th: International Classification of Diseases, Tenth Revision, Clinical Modification (ICD-10-CM) Code

section.heading: Sensitive Health Services.
section.text: The Secretary shall determine what sensitive health services are subject to restriction under this chapter based on the recommendations of the Protected Health Care Commission.

para.text: In COMAR 10.29.17 — COMAR 10.29.20, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.

“Advertising” means the publication, dissemination, or circulation of any oral or written matter, including labeling, which directly or indirectly calls to the attention of the public the goods and services one has to offer.
“Alkaline hydrolysis” means the process of reducing human remains using water, alkaline chemicals, and heat inside a watertight vessel to accelerate decomposition.
“Authorizing agent” means an individual who has legal authority to arrange for and make decisions regarding the final disposition of human remains, including cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.
“Board” means Board of Morticians and Funeral Directors.
“Consumer” includes a funeral establishment contracting with a crematory or the authorizing agent of a decedent contracting with a crematory.
“Cremated human remains” means the recoverable bone fragments of a deceased person that result from the completion of cremation.
“Cremation” means the disposition of a dead human body by means of incineration.
“Cremator” means the machinery within which the process of cremation begins.
“Crematory” means a building, portion of a building, or structure that houses the necessary appliances and facilities for cremation, alkaline hydrolysis, or both.
“Crematory container” means a readily combustible, rigid container in which human remains are sent to the crematory and then placed in the cremator for cremation.
“Crematory equipment” means either:
A cremator; or
A hydrolysis unit.
“Decedent” means a dead human being.
“Embalming” means the disinfection and preserving of human remains by arterial or cavity injection or any other type of preservation.
“Engage in the operation of a crematory” means controlling or managing a crematory.
“Holding facility” means an area, within or adjacent to a crematory, designed for the retention of human remains before cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.
Human Remains.
“Human remains” means:
The body of a deceased person; or
A part of a body or limb that has been removed from a living person.
“Human remains” includes the body or part of a body or limb in any state of decomposition.

“Hydrolysis container” means a container, including a body bag, in which human remains are sent to the crematory for alkaline hydrolysis, but the human remains may be removed prior to the start of the process for alkaline hydrolysis.
“Hydrolysis unit” means the watertight vessel in which the process of alkaline hydrolysis occurs.
“Hydrolyzed human remains” means the recoverable bone fragments of a deceased person that result from the completion of alkaline hydrolysis.
“Office” means the Office of Cemetery Oversight.
“Permit” means a license issued by the Board to allow a person to operate a business which engages in the operation of a crematory or to act as a registered crematory operator.
“Permit holder” means the holder of a permit issued under Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland, to operate a crematory or to act as a registered crematory operator.

“Person” means an individual, personal representative, or a corporation that is the operator of a mortuary science business where the practice of mortuary science is conducted for the corporation by a licensed mortician or funeral director, a partnership, or professional association, each comprised of one or more licensed morticians or funeral directors.
“Processed human remains” means the end result of pulverization.

“Pulverization” means the process following cremation or alkaline hydrolysis of crushing, grinding, or milling cremated or hydrolyzed human remains into fine particles or powder, leaving bone fragments reduced to 5 millimeters or less.
“Registered crematory operator” means an individual registered to operate a crematory as a sole proprietor or on behalf of a sole proprietor or permit holder.

“Responsible party” means a sole proprietor or the individual designated by a corporation, limited liability company, or partnership, which holds a permit to engage in the operation of a crematory, to be responsible for the operations of the crematory.
“Sealable container” means a container in which processed human remains can be placed and sealed so as to prevent leakage of the processed human remains or the entry of any foreign material.

annotations.annotation: Business Regulation Article, §5-101; Health-General Article, §5-508; Health Occupations Article, §§7-101, 7-102, and 7-205, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: April 14, 2014 (41:7 Md. R. 421)
Regulation .02 amended effective May 11, 2026 (53:9 Md. R. 413)

para.text: Office of Cemetery Oversight. An individual shall obtain a permit to engage in the operation of a crematory from the Office:
If the individual:
Holds a permit or registration under Business Regulation Article, Title 5, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
Owns a greater interest in a crematory than a licensee or holder of a corporation license under Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland;

If the person’s ownership interest is equal to the ownership interest in the crematory of a licensee or holder of a corporation license under Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
If the person who owns the crematory is not:
A licensee or holder of a corporation license under Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
A registrant or permit holder under Business Regulation Article, Title 5, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A permit issued by the Office shall specify whether the crematory is authorized to offer and perform either cremation services, alkaline hydrolysis services, or both.
Board of Morticians and Funeral Directors. A person shall obtain a permit to engage in the operation of a crematory from the Board if the person:
Is a licensee or holder of a corporation license under Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
Owns a greater interest in a crematory than a person who holds a permit or registration under Business Regulation Article, Title 5, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Before obtaining a permit from the Board, a crematory may cremate or hydrolyze the human remains of one decedent provided the:

Cremation or hydrolysis is only performed as part of the manufacturer’s operator training course for the cremation machinery or the hydrolysis unit in which the cremation or hydrolysis takes place; and
The person or persons with the right to final disposition of the decedent are notified and have given permission before the cremation or the alkaline hydrolysis occurs.
Subject to approval of the Board, a person shall receive a permit to operate a crematory in this State if the person meets the permit requirements of this chapter.
A person shall:
Submit an application on a form provided by the Board;
Pay the non-refundable application fee and any other applicable fees set forth in Regulation .08 of this chapter;
Be of good moral character if the applicant is an individual;

Designate a registered crematory operator who shall be responsible for the day-to-day operation of the business provided that the registered crematory operator may not be designated as the responsible party for more than a total of two crematories;
Provide a certificate of status, issued by the Maryland Department of Assessments and Taxation, if the applicant is a business entity:
Indicating that the entity is in good standing, or its equivalent as determined by the Board; and
Dated not earlier than 30 days before the application; and
Comply with all applicable federal, State, and local laws.
An operating crematory shall apply to the Board for a permit within 90 days from the effective date of this regulation or be subject to Board action.
Before applying to the Board for a permit, any crematory not in operation before the effective date of this chapter shall have in its employ an individual who holds a certification from:
The Cremation Association of North America (CANA);
The International Cemetery, Cremation and Funeral Association (ICCFA); or
Another equivalent body recognized jointly by the Board and the Office.

section.heading: Requirements for Registration of a Registered Crematory Operator.

para.text: An individual may be registered by the Board as a crematory operator if the individual meets the requirements of this regulation.

The permit issued by the Board shall specify whether the individual’s training and registration required by this chapter permits the individual to operate a cremator, an alkaline hydrolysis unit, or both.
An individual shall:
Submit an application on a form provided by the Board;
Pay the non-refundable fee and any other applicable fee set forth in Regulation .08 of this chapter;
Be of good moral character;
Be 18 years old or older;
Achieve certification as a crematory operator by the:
Cremation Association of North America (CANA);
International Cemetery, Cremation and Funeral Association (ICCFA); or
Other equivalent certification recognized jointly by the Board and the Office; and
In the crematory with which the applicant is affiliated:
Successfully complete the operator training course of the manufacturer of the cremator or the alkaline hydrolysis unit located in the crematory with which the applicant is affiliated; or
Provide a notarized letter by the supervising crematory operator for which the individual is employed indicating the completion of training on the cremator or the alkaline hydrolysis unit.
To qualify as a trainer, the supervising crematory operator shall have a minimum of 3 years’ experience on the associated cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit.
Before the expiration date of the permit or a registration, a permit holder or a registered crematory operator shall:
Complete and return the renewal form;
Pay the non-refundable renewal fee set forth in Regulation .08 of this chapter; and
Submit any required documentation to the Board.

A person who has a permit or registration as a permit holder or registered crematory operator may renew the permit or registration every 2 years, beginning on a date specified by the Board, in the following manner:
Complete the renewal application form;
Pay the non-refundable renewal fee set forth in Regulation .08 of this chapter;
Meet the permit and registration requirements of this chapter and Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
Submit the required documentation.

After the expiration date, if an application for renewal of a permit or registration has not been made or if the individual has been identified by the Office of the Comptroller or the Maryland Department of Labor as delinquent in the payment of taxes or unemployment insurance contributions, pursuant to tax compliance regulations of COMAR 10.31.02 or, pursuant to Family Law Article, §10-119.3, Annotated Code of Maryland, has outstanding child support obligations, the individual may not engage in the operation of a crematory business or act as a registered crematory operator.

An individual who engages in the operation of a crematory business or acts as a registered crematory operator without renewing the required permit or registration is subject to disciplinary action by the Board.

If a permit holder or a registered crematory operator applies for renewal past the expiration date of the permit or registration, the individual shall pay the renewal fee plus the late renewal fee set forth in Regulation .08 of this chapter.

section.heading: Lapsed Permit.
section.text: A person who has been issued a permit and who has allowed the permit to lapse for at least 6 months may apply for a permit by doing the following:

para.text: Completing a permit application form;
Paying the permit fee and the late renewal fee set forth in Regulation .08 of this chapter;
Meeting the permit requirements under this chapter;
Submitting the documentation required by this chapter;
Providing a full written explanation to the Board detailing the reasons why the permit was allowed to expire and why a permit is now sought; and
Submitting to the Board an affidavit stating that the person did not engage in the operation of a crematory in this State while the permit was lapsed.

annotations.annotation: Business Regulation Article, §§5-101, 5-204, and 5-303; Health Occupations Article, §§1-213, 7-101, 7-102, 7-205, 7-314—7-316, and 7-319, Annotated Code of Maryland
