Regulation .07B amended effective May 11, 2026 (53:9 Md. R. 413)

para.text: A crematory shall be available for inspection by a representative of the Board at any time during operating hours.
A crematory shall be inspected:
On at least a biennial basis;
In furtherance of an investigation; or
Upon the sale or change of ownership of the crematory.

The permit holder or any person responsible for day-to-day operation of the cremation machinery or the alkaline hydrolysis unit shall be available to accompany the inspector during the inspection and sign the initial inspection report.
A copy of the inspection report shall be provided to the permit holder or the registered crematory operator responsible for the day-to-day operation of the crematory.
A crematory shall maintain the following minimum standards:
The premises shall be maintained in a sanitary manner to comply with Centers for Disease Control’s guidelines on universal precautions;

Except by express written consent of the authorizing agents to perform simultaneous, multiple cremations or alkaline hydrolyses, there may not be more than one human body cremated or hydrolyzed simultaneously in a single cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit at a time;
A cremator or an alkaline hydrolysis unit shall be completely cleaned after each cremation or alkaline hydrolysis;
There may not be co-mingling of human remains and pet remains in:
Refrigeration units;
Cremators; or
Alkaline hydrolysis units;
Separate cremators or alkaline hydrolysis units shall be dedicated for the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis of human remains and the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis of pets;
Except as provided in §E(2) of this regulation, there may not be:
Co-mingling mingling of processed human remains with other processed human remains or pet remains;
Scooping of processed human remains from a bucket containing co-mingled processed human remains
Any form of misrepresentation in the return of processed human remains;

Excluding a deceased person with a known communicable infection, human remains shall be properly identified before cremation or alkaline hydrolysis by verifying that the documentation accompanying the human remains is consistent with a visual observation of the human remains;
Burial transit permit shall be an original document pertaining to the deceased;
Each crematory shall have:
A sink with hot and cold water within the work area housing the crematory equipment; and
The tools necessary to thoroughly clean the floor within the work area housing the crematory equipment with water and an appropriate sanitizing agent;
The name of the deceased shall be visible on the outside of the crematory container;
Human remains shall be properly stored before cremation or alkaline hydrolysis;
Crematories shall:
Use only mechanical pulverizing equipment meeting industry standards that has been totally brushed as clean as possible between each use; and
Pulverize the processed human remains with a mortar and pestle if the processed human remains are not sufficient in amount for pulverizing in mechanical equipment;
Separate pulverizing drums shall be dedicated for processed human remains and processed pet remains;
Processed human remains may not be held pending payment of any fees;

Within 6 months of the effective date of these regulations, cremators without safety devices that prevent the automatic door from dropping unintentionally shall be retrofitted with such safety devices;
Portable fans may not be used in the area of the cremator;
There shall be a minimum of 6 inches between cremator smoke stack and the roof of the crematory;
Before pulverizing, the mechanical pulverizer shall be clamped;
A cremator shall have visible on its front a warning sign stating, “No leaning past the door of the cremator.”;
The permit holder or registered crematory operator shall be present at the crematory during the operation of the:
Cremator;
Alkaline hydrolysis unit; or
Pulverizer; and
All individuals:

Who operate the cremator or the alkaline hydrolysis unit in a crematory shall be certified by the Cremation Association of North America (CANA), International Cemetery, Cremation, and Funeral Association (ICCFA), or other equivalent certification recognized jointly by the Board and the Office; and

Receiving training toward certification to operate a cremator or an alkaline hydrolysis unit shall be allowed to work under the supervision of a registered crematory operator who has the required certification for a period of at least 6 months.
The following completed forms from previous cremations or alkaline hydrolysis shall be available at all times for inspection and copying and are subject to be pulled at random by the inspector:
Crematory services authorization form;
Burial transit permit;
Delegation of authority form;
Receipt for human remains;
Record of cremation or alkaline hydrolysis;
Certificate of cremation or alkaline hydrolysis; and
Return of processed human remains certificate.
Inspection results shall be written on forms approved by the Board.
The Board representative performing the inspection shall, on completion of the inspection:
Apprise the permit holder of the findings of the inspection; and
Provide the permit holder or representative of the permit holder with a copy of the inspection report.
The permit holder or representative of the permit holder shall sign and verify receipt of the inspection report.
At the time of the Board inspection, the permit holder shall provide written documentation to the Board’s inspector that:
The crematory equipment has been inspected in accordance with manufacturer specifications;
In accordance with manufacturer specifications, it is not time for a manufacturer’s inspection; or
The permit holder has requested that the manufacturer conduct an inspection of the crematory equipment.

section.heading: Crematory Inspection Report, Deficiencies, and Penalties.

para.text: The Board shall review the inspection report and make a determination as to whether a deficiency exists.
The Board shall notify the permit holder of the results of the inspection by providing a copy of the inspection report for the permit holder.

If a permit holder passes an inspection, the permit holder shall, prominently and in public view, display on the premises a statement issued by the Board that the crematory has successfully passed an inspection.
If the Board finds a deficiency, the Board shall:
Within 7 days, notify the permit holder of the deficiency or failure to pass the inspection; and
Provide a copy of the inspection report to the permit holder within 30 days.
Correction of Deficiency.
Except as provided in this section, a permit holder shall correct a deficiency within 30 days after receipt of notification or sooner as determined by the Board.
The Board may require immediate correction of a deficiency if the Board considers the correction necessary in the interest of public health.
The permit holder may request an extension of time for correction of a deficiency.
The Board may approve an extension of time for correction of a deficiency.
Upon completion of correction of all deficiencies, the permit holder shall notify the Board.
Upon notification from the permit holder, a representative of the Board shall re-inspect the crematory.
The Board shall notify the permit holder in writing of the results of the re-inspection by providing a copy of a new inspection report to the permit holder.

If re-inspection reveals additional deficiencies not cited in the first report, the permit holder shall correct those deficiencies in the time period specified by the Board unless an extension of time is requested by the permit holder and approved by the Board.

Penalties. In the event that a permit holder fails to allow an inspection of the crematory or fails to comply with a notice to correct deficiencies or violations within the time specified, the Board shall:
Deem the failure to be a threat to the public health, safety, or welfare and requiring emergency action;
Summarily suspend the crematory permit as provided under State Government Article, §10-226(c)(2), Annotated Code of Maryland;
Provide notice of the Board’s action to the permit holder; and
Provide the permit holder an opportunity to be heard.

section.heading: Grounds for Discipline, Hearing, and Penalties.

para.text: Pursuant to the provisions of Health Occupations Article, §§7-316 and 7-319, Annotated Code of Maryland, the Board may:
Deny an applicant:
A crematory permit; or
A crematory operator registration;
Reprimand any:
Permit holder; or
Registered crematory operator; or
Suspend or revoke:
A crematory permit; or
A crematory operator registration.

Pursuant to the provisions of Health Occupations Article, §7-317, Annotated Code of Maryland, if the Board, after bringing an action, finds that there are grounds for probation, suspension, or revocation, the Board may impose a penalty not exceeding $5,000.

annotations.annotation: Health-General Article, §§5-502—5-504, 5-508, and 5-511—5-514; Health Occupations Article, §§7-101, 7-102, 7-205, 7-316, 7-317, 7-319, and 7-406, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: April 14, 2014 (41:7 Md. R. 421)
Regulation .02 amended effective May 11, 2026 (53:9 Md. R. 413)
Regulation .03B, E amended effective May 11, 2026 (53:9 Md. R. 413)
Regulation .06A amended effective May 11, 2026 (53:9 Md. R. 413)

section.heading: Scope.
section.text: This chapter governs the procedures to be followed in performing a cremation or alkaline hydrolysis in this State.

section.heading: Removal, Identification, and Transportation of Human Remains.

para.text: Upon receipt of human remains, a permit holder in the presence of the authorizing agent or representative of the funeral establishment engaging crematory services shall:
Verify that the information on the wrist tag is consistent with the:
Documentation accompanying the deceased; and
Visual observation of the remains themselves;
Use a metal detector wand to inspect for the presence of any battery operated, implanted devices including pacemakers, defibrillators, or pain relief devices;
Refuse to accept human remains for cremation if an inspection of the human remains indicates the presence of any battery operated, implanted device;

Ensure the removal and properly dispose of any hazardous object or any other materials that the individual authorized under this subtitle deems should be removed from the human remains, the crematory container, or the hydrolysis container, in order to prevent harm to the public health or damage to the cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit;
Remove any jewelry:
On the human remains;
In the crematory container; or
In the hydrolysis container;
Return any removed jewelry to the authorizing agent or representative of the funeral establishment engaging crematory services; and

Obtain a signed, itemized receipt from the authorizing agent or representative of the funeral establishment engaging crematory services for the removed jewelry and retain the receipt as a permanent record.
Foreign objects removed from the human remains:
Shall be treated as medical waste and disposed of accordingly; and
May not be donated until a sterilization process through a third party recognized by the Board to dispose properly of medical waste has been performed.
Materials identifying the human remains that are placed in the custody of a permit holder shall contain the following information about the decedent:
Name;
Date of birth;
Date of death;
Name of funeral establishment or authorizing agent;
Gender; and
Name and relationship of authorizing agent to deceased.
A permit holder may not accept unidentified human remains for cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.
A crematory container:
Shall be a readily combustible, rigid container suitable for cremation;
Shall provide a completely enclosed covering for the human remains;
Shall be resistant to leakage or spillage;
Shall be of sufficient strength and rigidity for ease of handling;
Shall provide protection to the health and safety of crematory establishment personnel and the public;
Shall comply with all local, State, and federal governmental emissions regulations;
May not be composed of metal or polyethylene material; and
Which appears to be heavily coated with varnish, lacquer, or any other highly combustible substance, shall be:
Placed in a cold cremator; or
Coated completely with water before being placed in the cremator.
An alkaline hydrolysis container:
Shall consist of a completely enclosed covering for the human remains;
Shall be resistant to leakage or spillage;
Shall provide protection to the health and safety of the crematory establishment personnel and the public;
Shall comply with all local, State, and federal emissions regulations; and
Once inside the alkaline hydrolysis unit, may be removed prior to the start of the alkaline hydrolysis process as long as the human remains are covered to protect the dignity of the decedent.

Unless prevented by emergency circumstances, human remains that have been designated for cremation or alkaline hydrolysis shall be cremated or hydrolyzed by a permit holder within 48 hours after receipt.

A permit holder may not hold human remains for cremation or alkaline hydrolysis unless the human remains are contained within an individual, closed cremation or hydrolysis container as required under Regulation .03 of this chapter.
A permit holder may not accept a cremation or alkaline hydrolysis container from which there is any evidence of leakage of the body fluids from the human remains therein.

Whenever a permit holder is unable to cremate or hydrolyze human remains within 48 hours of taking custody thereof due to emergency circumstances, the permit holder shall maintain the human remains in a refrigerated holding facility, either on or off site, at 40°F or less, unless the remains have been embalmed.

section.heading: Identification of Human Remains Immediately Before Cremation or Alkaline Hydrolysis.

para.text: Immediately before being placed within the cremator or the alkaline hydrolysis unit, the individual authorized under this subtitle shall:
Verify the identification of the human remains; and

Place in or on the cremator or the alkaline hydrolysis unit the hard metal or plastic identification disc of the human remains being processed where it shall remain in place until the cremation or the alkaline hydrolysis process is complete.
The permit holder’s designee may not leave the:
Immediate area of the cremator during the cremation process; or
Facility for the alkaline hydrolysis unit during the alkaline hydrolysis process.
The alkaline hydrolysis unit shall be monitored at all times during the alkaline hydrolysis process.
Records.

The permit holder shall maintain the written authorizations required by Regulation .08 of this chapter as required pursuant to Health-General Article, §4-304, Annotated Code of Maryland, and COMAR 10.01.16.04B.
The records are subject to inspection and copying by the Board.
Except as otherwise provided in COMAR 10.29.18.02, a permit holder may not cremate or hydrolyze human remains until:
The body has been identified as required under Health Occupation Article, §7-411, Annotated Code of Maryland;
The permit holder crematory has received:
A crematory services authorization on a form approved by the Board and signed by an authorizing agent;
If applicable, a written delegation document or facsimile; and
Any other documentation required by federal, State, or local law; and
The permit holder has documented that at least 12 hours have elapsed from the time of death of the individual prior to beginning cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.
The crematory services authorization form shall:
Be provided by the permit holder to the authorizing agent;
Contain the following information:
The identity of the decedent;
Date of death;
The name and address of the authorizing agent and the relationship between the authorizing agent and the deceased;
Authorization for the permit holder to cremate or hydrolyze the human remains;
An indication of whether the decedent is:
A veteran, as defined in State Government Article, § 9-901, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
An eligible dependent of a veteran, as defined in Business Regulation Article, §5-803, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Authorization to verify that the following have been removed before cremation or alkaline hydrolysis:
An implanted pacemaker or defibrillator; and
Any other materials that should be removed to prevent harm to the public health or damage to equipment;
A representation by the authorizing agent that radiologic implant treatment has not occurred within 5 days before cremation or alkaline hydrolysis;
A representation that the authorizing agent is aware of no objection to the human remains being processed by any person who has a right to control the disposition of the human remains;
The name and address of the person authorized to claim the processed human remains from the permit holder or to accept the processed remains via registered mail; and
The name and address of the crematory to which the authorizing agent has given permission; and
Be signed by the authorizing agent.
If an authorizing agent is not readily available to execute the crematory services authorization form, the authorizing agent may transmit the document in writing with notary seal electronically:
In writing; or
If located outside the area, by transmitting to the permit holder:

A signed statement electronically or by facsimile that contains the name, address, and relationship of the sender to the decedent and the name and address of the individual to whom authority is delegated; and
Once the authorizing agent is located in the area, a notarized document through U.S. Postal Service attesting to the delegation of authority.

Upon receipt of the written delegation document or a copy transmitted electronically or by facsimile, the permit holder shall allow the named individual to serve as the authorizing agent. The documents shall be signed by the authorizing agent in the presence of a notary.

A person signing a crematory services authorization form is deemed to warrant the truthfulness of any facts set forth in the crematory services authorization form, including the identity of the deceased whose remains are sought to be processed and the authority of that person to order such a cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.

A permit holder shall notify and obtain written consent of the authorizing agent before the cremation or the alkaline hydrolysis of human remains at a location other than the location named on the crematory services authorization form.
A permit holder shall maintain a copy of every crematory services authorization form required under this regulation as permanent records.
Crematory services authorization records are subject to inspection and copying by the Board.

A permit holder shall provide to an individual who delivers human remains for cremation or alkaline hydrolysis a receipt signed by both the permit holder authority and the individual who delivered the human remains, that includes the:
Name of the individual from whom the human remains were received and the name of the individual’s employer, if any;
Name and address of the crematory;
Name of the deceased;
Gender of the deceased;
Date of death of the deceased; and
Verification of the authorized burial transit permit.
The permit holder shall maintain a record of each cremation or alkaline hydrolysis, which shall include the:
Name of the decedent;
Date of birth of the decedent;
Gender of the decedent;
Date of death;
Name and address of the authorizing agent;
Date, time, and location of cremation or alkaline hydrolysis; and
Name of the individual who performed the cremation or the alkaline hydrolysis.

The permit holder shall provide a certificate of disposition of processed human remains to the authorizing agent or funeral establishment that arranged for the cremation or the alkaline hydrolysis that includes the:
Name of the decedent;
Name of the authorizing agent;
Name and address of the person who received the processed human remains from the permit holder; and
If ascertainable:
The location, including the name of the cemetery and plot location if the remains are interred; and
The manner and date of the disposition of the processed human remains.
The permit holder shall maintain a copy of every record and receipt required by this chapter as permanent records.
All records and receipts required by this chapter are subject to inspection and copying by the Board.
On completion of the cremation or the alkaline hydrolysis, insofar as is possible:
All of the recoverable residue of the cremation or the alkaline hydrolysis process shall be:
Removed from the cremator or the alkaline hydrolysis unit;
Except for medical devices remaining after cremation or alkaline hydrolysis, pulverized; and
Placed in a sealable container; and
The identification disc required by Health Occupations Article, §7-411, Annotated Code of Maryland, shall be:
Removed from the cremator or the alkaline hydrolysis unit; and
Placed in the sealable container with the processed human remains.
Medical devices which remain after the completion of the cremation or the alkaline hydrolysis process shall be treated as medical waste and disposed of accordingly.
After pulverization, all of the processed human remains, together with the identification disc, shall be placed in a sealable container.

If the sealable container opening is not of adequate dimensions to accommodate a disc, it shall be affixed to the exterior of the sealable container and a record of the cremation number shall be placed in the sealable container.

If all of the processed human remains and the identification disc will not fit within the dimensions of a sealable container, the remainder of the remains shall be returned to the authorizing agent, or the agent’s representative, in a separate, sealable container. Container seams shall be taped.

If the processed human remains and identification disc do not adequately fill the container’s interior dimensions, the extra space may be filled with packing material that will not become intermingled with the remains and then securely closed.
If a sealable container is to be shipped, it shall:
Be placed within a separate sturdy box with all box seams securely taped closed; and
Have the name of the deceased person whose processed remains are contained therein clearly identified on the outside of the container.

If processed human remains have been in the possession of a permit holder, as originally authorized by the authorizing agent without instructions for disposition for a period of 10 days or more from the date of cremation or alkaline hydrolysis, the permit holder may send the processed human remains, using the current mailing standard approved by the U.S. Postal Service, return receipt requested, to the authorizing agent.
Veteran and Eligible Dependent Decedents.

Unless the authorizing agent directs otherwise, if a permit holder in possession of processed remains of a decedent veteran or a decedent eligible dependent, the permit holder shall provide the decedent’s identifying information to a veterans service organization, as defined in Business Regulation Article, §5-803, Annotated Code of Maryland.
The veterans service organization will notify the permit holder whether the decedent is a veteran or an eligible dependent eligible for burial in a veterans cemetery.

If the unclaimed processed remains are those of a veteran or an eligible dependent, the permit holder may transfer the processed remains to a veterans service organization for the purpose of disposition of the processed remains.

If a veterans service organization does not take possession of unclaimed processed remains that qualify for a plot in a State veterans cemetery under State Government Article, §9-906, Annotated Code of Maryland, within 10 days after the permit holder receives the notification required in this regulation, the permit holder shall:
Notify the Department of Veterans and Military Families of the status of the processed remains for the purpose of the appropriate disposition of the processed remains; and
Transfer the processed remains to the Department of Veterans and Military Families for the purpose of the appropriate disposition of the processed remains.
A crematory offering cremation shall maintain, at its own expense, the following tool inventory:
Safety placement tool measuring a minimum of 49 inches in length;
Wire brushes for cleaning cremators;
Brush with fine bristles for cleaning pulverizers;
Mortar and pestle;
Funnel;
High temperature protective gloves and heat-resistant leather gloves;
Hand magnet and metal detector wand to detect and remove metal from cremated human remains;
Tweezers to remove nonmetal objects from cremated human remains; and
National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health certified N95 filtering facepiece respirators.
A crematory offering alkaline hydrolysis shall maintain, at its own expense, the following tool inventory:
Suitable tools for cleaning the alkaline hydrolysis unit;
Brush with fine bristles for cleaning pulverizers;
Mortar and pestle;
Funnel;
Hand magnet and metal detector wand to detect and remove metal from hydrolyzed human remains;
Tweezers to remove nonmetal objects from hydrolyzed human remains;
National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health certified N95 filtering facepiece respirators; and

An emergency shower and eyewash combination unit meeting the criteria established under the American National Standards Institute and the International Safety Equipment Association Z358.1-2014 (R2020) or its successor.
The permit holder or the designated crematory operator shall:

Submit to the Board the number of persons not affiliated with the operations of the crematory who can safely be within the same room and within 10 feet of the cremator door or the alkaline hydrolysis unit; and
Verify to the Board that the permit holder has submitted the information required in §A(1) of this regulation to their liability insurance carrier.

Crematories that begin operation after July 1, 2014, shall construct a viewing room in the area of the cremator if the crematory intends to have more than two visitors present in the area of the cremator during the cremation.

Crematories providing alkaline hydrolysis shall construct a viewing room in the area of the alkaline hydrolysis unit if the crematory intends to have more than two visitors present in the area of the alkaline hydrolysis unit during the alkaline hydrolysis.

annotations.annotation: Health-General Article, §§5-502—5-504, 5-508, and 5-511—5-514; Health Occupations Article, §§7-101, 7-102, and 7-205, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: April 14, 2014 (41:7 Md. R. 421)
Chapter revised effective May 11, 2026 (53:9 Md. R. 413)

para.text: A permit holder or registered crematory operator shall:
Act in a manner that respects and protects the dignity of a decedent and the decedent’s family;
Conduct business in a reasonable, usual, and customary manner avoiding unfair trade practices;
Comply with the State public health laws as set forth in Health-General Article, §§4-215 and 5-501, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Comply with the Maryland Morticians and Funeral Directors Act, Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Implement and follow through on all arrangements agreed on between consumer and the crematory;

Provide appropriate services for and respect the rights of individuals without regard to age, race, creed, national origin, gender, disability, marital status, political belief, religious affiliation, social or economic status, or social preferences;
Comply with all local, State, and federal laws regarding the final disposition of human remains;
Be sensitive and responsive to the bereavement needs of a decedent’s family; and
Provide a general price list in effect at that time in accordance with the Federal Trade Commission’s funeral rule, 16 CFR part 453.
In advertising, a licensee may not include statements:
That are misrepresentations of facts;
That are likely to mislead or deceive because, in context, the statement makes only a partial disclosure of relevant facts;
Relating to fees without reasonable disclosure of all relevant variables so that the statement would not be misunderstood or be deceptive to a consumer; or
Containing representations or implications that in reasonable probability can be expected to cause an ordinarily prudent individual to misunderstand or be deceived.
A permit holder or crematory operator may not:
Use, or participate in the use of, any form of communication to consumers containing a false, fraudulent, misleading, deceptive, or unfair statement or claim; or
Operate a crematory, or perform a cremation or alkaline hydrolysis while under the influence of alcohol, an illegal drug, or a controlled dangerous substance, without the prescription of a physician.

annotations.annotation: Health Occupations Article, §7-101, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: April 14, 2014 (41:7 Md. R. 421)
Regulation .02C amended effective May 11, 2026 (53:9 Md. R. 413)

section.heading: Temperature of Refrigeration Units.
section.text: A refrigeration unit used in the storage of human remains shall be kept at a temperature lower than 40 degrees Fahrenheit.

annotations.annotation: Health-General Article, §5-513; Health Occupations Article, §7-205, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: August 5, 2013 (40:15 Md. R. 1229)
Regulation .01 amended effective May 11, 2026 (53:9 Md. R. 413)

codify:replace-or-insert.heading: OFFICE OF THE CHIEF MEDICAL EXAMINER

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Autopsy report” means a written document detailing the medical findings and conclusions of a postmortem examination performed by a medical examiner.
“First degree relative” means a surviving spouse, parent, grandparent, sibling, and child of the deceased.
“Forensic investigator” means a chief forensic investigator, deputy chief forensic investigator, lead forensic investigator, or forensic investigator who:
Is employed under or appointed by the authority of the Maryland Department of Health;
Assists those medical examiners in §B(4)(a) of this regulation;
Has education, training, or experience in trauma or death investigation; and
Is appointed in accordance with Health-General Article, §5-306, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Medical Examiner.

“Medical examiner” means a Chief Medical Examiner, Deputy Chief Medical Examiner, or assistant medical examiner who is a pathologist authorized to carry out the provisions of Health-General Article, §5-308, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Medical examiner” also means a deputy medical examiner who:
Assists those examiners in §B(4)(a) of this regulation;
Is a physician; and
Is appointed by the Maryland Department of Health in accordance with Health-General Article, §§5-305 and 5-306, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Medical Examiner Case.
“Medical examiner case” means any death which occurs as described in Health-General Article, §5-308(a), Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Medical examiner case” does not mean:

A stillbirth or a neonatal death, or accident room or hospital death in which the cause of death has been established by the hospital physician and is due to disease, and free of evidence of criminal or accidental nature;
A case which is dead on arrival at the hospital and the physician who pronounces death has been in previous attendance on the patient; or
A death which occurs in a hospital within 24 hours of admission merely because the death occurred within 24 hours.
“Procurement organization” has the meaning stated in Estates and Trusts Article, §4-501, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Secretary” means the Secretary of Health.

section.heading: Report of Medical Examiner Cases to the Police or Sheriff.

para.text: Whenever a physician, funeral director, or other person has knowledge of a death that may fall under the jurisdiction of the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner in accordance with Health-General Article, §5-308(a), Annotated Code of Maryland, the physician, funeral director, or other person having this knowledge shall report it without delay to the police or sheriff law enforcement agency who has jurisdiction in the city or county where the death occurred.

The medical examiner or forensic investigator may, in conjunction with law enforcement or under court authority, investigate any location believed to be associated with the death to gather essential facts to determine cause of death or manner of death.
A health care facility may not refuse the medical examiner access to a body which is the subject of a medical examiner investigation at any time.

section.heading: Medical Examiner.
section.text: Any death in accordance with Health-General Article, §5-308(a), Annotated Code of Maryland, in the State shall be investigated and examined by the medical examiner in the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner in Baltimore, or in any other place as may be approved by the Chief Medical Examiner.

section.heading: Establishing a Hospital Death as a Medical Examiner Case.

para.text: The hospital physician shall be guided by the history and other circumstances in judging whether or not a death is under the jurisdiction of the medical examiner.
Cases that do not fall under medical examiner jurisdiction may not be made such and will not be accepted simply because hospital autopsy permission has been refused or is not available.

Cases that fall under medical examiner jurisdiction may not be autopsied by the hospital pathologist because consent for autopsy has been obtained in accordance with Health-General Article, §5-501, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Appointment.

Each hospital shall appoint a responsible agent, office, or individual with whom the medical examiner can communicate at any hour regarding medical examiner cases in the hospital, after receiving notification of them from the police authority.
Each hospital shall notify the medical examiner of the agent so appointed.
Responsibilities.

Notification of Police. In accordance with Regulation .02 of this chapter, whenever a hospital in Maryland has a medical examiner case, the hospital agent shall report the case to the local law enforcement agency with jurisdiction in that subdivision of the State. The local law enforcement agency so notified shall transmit the information to the medical examiner’s investigator on duty.

A health care facility may not refuse the medical examiner access to a body which is the subject of a medical examiner’s case at any time. In order to prevent destruction or alteration of evidence, once death is determined, the hospital shall follow medical examiner guidance as to management of the body of the deceased. The medical examiner shall respond to the location of the body, investigate the case, and arrange for transportation of the body.

Preliminary Hospital Reports. The hospital agent designated in accordance with Regulation .08 of this chapter shall submit promptly to the medical examiner, for each medical examiner case, the following information:
The name and age, if known, of each decedent;
The date and time of admission;
Time of death;
Diagnosis, if made;
Place, date, time, and manner of accident, or violence, if any, and other information that the Medical Examiner may request;
Next-of-Kin, if known, as an attempt of a reasonable search effort; and
If any evidence is recovered during therapeutic intervention, the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner shall be notified.

Medical Examiner Requests. The hospital agent shall promptly respond to a medical examiner request for records and specimens. Failure to provide requested information within 24 hours will result in a report to the Office of Health Care Quality.
If, during a routine autopsy by a hospital pathologist, evidence is encountered which indicates that the death should be under the jurisdiction of the medical examiner:
The death then shall become a medical examiner case;
The hospital agent immediately shall notify the police or sheriff, who will in turn notify the medical examiner; and
The hospital pathologist shall discontinue the autopsy pending the arrival of the medical examiner.

Clinical and Autopsy Reports from Hospitals. Whenever a medical examiner case is autopsied in a hospital, the hospital agent shall submit to the medical examiner, without delay, a clinical summary report, preferably filled out by the physician most familiar with the case, and a provisional anatomical diagnosis, and later a copy of the final autopsy report.

section.heading: Tissue Recovery.
section.text: Whenever an authorized organ and tissue procurement organization has informed the Chief Medical Examiner or the Chief Medical Examiner's deputy or assistant medical examiner that there is a need for tissue for research or transplantation, the Chief Medical Examiner shall allow the authorized organ and tissue procurement organization to make a recovery if:

para.text: Proper authorization has been provided to the medical examiner;
The body has been tested to determine its suitability for transplantation;
An autopsy is going to be performed and the removal does not interfere with the subsequent investigation or examination; and
The medical examiner does not know of an objection to the autopsy or donation of tissue by the next of kin.

section.heading: Donation of Internal Organs for Transplantation.

para.text: Whenever there is an immediate need for an internal organ as a transplant, that is, heart, lung, kidney, liver, pancreas, spleen, lymph nodes, or adrenal gland, a medical examiner may provide the organ, if requested by a transplant surgeon, under the following conditions:
The medical examiner has jurisdiction over the body of a decedent;
The body has been tested to determine its suitability;
The hospital representative and treating physician have obtained proper authorization for this decedent;
A reasonable, unsuccessful search has been made by the procurement organization as required by Estates and Trusts Article, §4–512(f), Annotated Code of Maryland;
No objection by the next of kin is known or reasonably foreseen by the medical examiner; and
The removal of the organ requested for transplant does not interfere with an investigation or autopsy.

Whenever a medical examiner on duty determines that an organ cannot be removed for one of the reasons stated in Sec. A of this regulation, the transplant surgeon may request that this decision be reviewed by the Chief Medical Examiner, a Deputy Chief Medical Examiner, or their designee.

Whenever a decedent is determined to have died by some violent means, the medical examiner may not authorize removal of the requested organs without observation by the medical examiner or the medical examiner's agent.

section.heading: Administrative Review of Correction of Findings and Conclusions.

para.text: Except in a case of a finding of homicide, a person in interest as defined in General Provisions Article, §4-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, may request the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner to correct findings and conclusions on the cause and manner of death recorded on a certificate of death under General Provisions Article, §4-502, Annotated Code of Maryland within 180 days after a medical examiner files those findings and conclusions in accordance with Health-General Article, §5-309(d), Annotated Code of Maryland.
A person in interest, has the meaning stated in General Provisions Article, §4-101, Annotated Code of Maryland.
The request to correct the findings and conclusions on a death certificate shall:
Be in writing to the Chief Medical Examiner;
Describe the requested change precisely; and
State the reasons for the change.
Within 60 days after receiving the request in §C of this regulation, the Chief Medical Examiner shall provide the person in interest written notice of the action taken.

If the Chief Medical Examiner denies the request to correct findings and conclusions on the cause of death, the person in interest may appeal the denial in writing within 15 days to the Secretary. The Secretary shall refer the matter within 15 days of receipt to the Office of Administrative Hearings.
An administrative law judge shall conduct a hearing both on the denial and on the establishment of the findings and conclusions on the cause of death.
Upon reviewing the findings of fact submitted by an administrative law judge, the Secretary or the Secretary's designee shall issue an order within 60 days to:
Adopt the findings of the administrative law judge; or
Reject the findings of the administrative law judge and affirm the findings of the medical examiner.

If the Secretary or Secretary's designee rejects the findings of an administrative law judge, the person in interest may appeal that rejection to a circuit court of competent jurisdiction under Maryland law.

If the final decision of the Secretary or the Secretary's designee, or of a court of competent jurisdiction on appeal establishes a different finding or conclusion on the cause or manner of death of a deceased than that recorded on the certificate of death, the medical examiner shall amend the certificate to reflect the different finding or conclusion.

The medical examiner shall send a change letter to the Division of Vital Records to amend the certificate of death, to reflect the final decision of the Secretary or Secretary's designee, or a court of competent jurisdiction.

The final decision of the Secretary or the Secretary's designee, or of a court in an appeal under this regulation, may not give rise to any presumption concerning the application of any provision of or the resolution of any claim concerning a policy of insurance relating to the deceased.

If the findings of the medical examiner are upheld by the Secretary, the appellant is responsible for the costs of the contested case hearing, based on the billing rate established by the Office of Administrative Hearings. Otherwise, the Department is responsible for the costs.

Individual files of the Chief Medical Examiner are confidential medical records protected from disclosure under the provisions of Health-General Article, §4-301, Annotated Code of Maryland, and General Provisions Article, §§4-329 et. seq., Annotated Code of Maryland.

An exception to these confidential medical records is the official medical examiner's autopsy report. This report is a public record and is generally subject to disclosure under General Provisions Article, §4-329, Annotated Code of Maryland, unless the case is subject to an ongoing investigation, or other appropriate reason for denial of disclosure exists.
Records request by court order or subpoena shall be subject to the fee schedule set forth herein.
Fee Schedule.
Autopsy reports may be sent electronically to the requestor. The fees schedule is the same for paper or electronic reports and is as follows:
No fee for a first-degree relative, as defined by COMAR 10.35.01.01B; and
A fee of $50 for all others may be charged for providing a requested autopsy report.

Requests for other information or material shall be accompanied by a court order or subpoena unless the Custodian of Records determines compelling circumstances exist warranting the release of materials. If releasable, the contents of the file will be copied at a cost of $1 per page with a minimum charge of $25 excluding any item independently listed in §D of this regulation.

Photographs are copied electronically. There is a $50 processing fee for each digital record, plus $5 per each image copied. X-rays and CT scans are copied electronically. There is a $50 processing fee for each digital record, plus $25 per X-ray.

Slides are copied electronically. There is a $50 processing fee for each digital record, plus $20 for each image copied. Slides may be viewed in the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner facility at a rate of $200 for the first hour and $50 for every 15 minute increment thereafter, partial or whole. The reviewer will have access to the medical examiner during the review.
A proof of death document may be obtained for $20.
If any or all of the items listed in §D of this regulation are requested in digital format, an additional fee of $60 shall apply.
These fees do not apply if the releasable documents are viewed only, by appointment, at the OCME, at the discretion of the custodian of records.

An individual, other than the custodian of the records of the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner or a designee, may not copy or distribute a copy of the official report of the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner.

section.heading: Child Abuse or Neglect, or Both.
section.text: A death that is investigated by the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner that is found to be caused by or related to child abuse or neglect shall be reported by a medical examiner orally and in writing to local departments of social services and the local law enforcement agency of the jurisdiction in which the child last resided, in accordance with Family Law Article, §5-704, Annotated Code of Maryland.

section.heading: Deaths in a State-Funded or State-Operated Facility.

para.text: A death in a State-funded or State-operated facility which constitutes a medical examiner's case, as defined in Health-General Article, §5-308, Annotated Code of Maryland, shall be investigated by the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner.

Notification and Investigation. The sheriff, police, or chief law enforcement officer, in the jurisdiction where a death occurs, shall notify the medical examiner whenever a death that constitutes a medical examiner's case occurs in a State-funded or State-operated facility. If the death may have occurred by violence, by suicide, by casualty, suddenly when the person was in apparently good health, not attended by a physician, or in any suspicious or unusual manner, the medical examiner or investigator shall:
Respond directly to the administrative head of the facility;
Conduct an investigation;
Complete the investigation report; and
Arrange to have the body sent to the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner for an autopsy, if necessary.
Completion of Investigation Report.
A medical examiner or investigator shall document in an investigation report all of the information regarding the death that was:
Gathered in the course of the investigation; and
Provided by the administrative head of the facility to the sheriff, police, chief law enforcement official, or health officer in the jurisdiction where the death occurred.

A medical examiner or investigator shall append to an investigation report the written report of the death that the administrative head of the facility provided to the sheriff, police, chief law enforcement official, or health officer in the jurisdiction where the death occurred.

Required Contents of Report. The report of the administrative head of a facility where the death occurred shall contain all the information set out in Health-General Article, §10-713, Annotated Code of Maryland. The report:
May be oral, if followed by a written report within 5 working days from the date of the death, or written; and
Shall contain the following relevant information:
The name, age, and sex of the deceased;
The time of discovery of the death;
The deceased's place of residence at the time of death;
If the death occurred in a place other than the residence of the deceased, the location of the body at the time of discovery;
The place where the body was found;
The name of the person who took custody of the body;
The name of the person evaluating the death, if known;
Whether an autopsy is being performed, if known;
The name, address, and telephone number of the next of kin or legal guardian, if known; and
Other information the administrative head of the facility determines should be provided to the medical examiner.

The report of the investigation by the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner constitutes an individual file of the Chief Medical Examiner not subject to disclosure under General Provisions Article, §4-101 et seq, Annotated Code of Maryland.
The Chief Medical Examiner shall appoint one or more deputy medical examiners and forensic investigators for each county.

The Chief Medical Examiner may appoint a deputy medical examiner from a list of qualified individuals submitted by the county medical society. However, the Chief Medical Examiner may appoint a deputy medical examiner for the county without a list if there is no county medical society or if the medical society does not submit a list of names.
Each deputy medical examiner appointed shall be a physician.
The Chief Medical Examiner shall select a forensic investigator from a list provided by the Deputy Chief Medical Examiner for Statewide services.
Entitled Fees.
As provided in the State budget, for each medical examiner’s case investigated, the deputy medical examiner or forensic investigator is entitled to a fee of $150.

If the case does not meet the requirements of Health-General Article, §5-308, Annotated Code of Maryland, and is declined, the deputy medical examiner or forensic investigator is entitled to a decline fee of $12.50.
The deputy medical examiner is entitled to $25 for certifying the death and completing the death certificate.
The deputy medical examiner or forensic investigator shall arrange for body transportation services of human remains from a scene of death in Maryland to a specified location within Maryland.

The deputy medical examiner or forensic investigator shall contact local county funeral directors or appropriate body transportation service providers within their jurisdiction, on a rotational basis, to arrange for body transportation services.

Funeral directors and body transportation service providers are eligible to transport human remains for the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner. However, it is the responsibility of each local funeral director or body transportation service to notify the deputy medical examiner or forensic investigator if they are interested in providing this service for the State.

Providers of body transportation services shall be available 24 hours a day, 7 days per week, 365 days per year to provide body transportation services, as needed, and shall provide their own equipment to transport human remains.

Human remains shall be accorded the proper dignity expected under accepted mortuary standards and practices, and shall be handled in accordance with standard precautions as defined by COMAR 10.06.01.02B and the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner guidelines to protect remains and evidence.
Payment.

Providers of body transportation services shall be paid as provided in the State budget, $3.50 per mile, one way, plus any tolls, and a scene response fee of $100, with a minimum total payment of $200 from the scene of death to a designated location. The mileage shall be verified by the OCME.
This is the sole compensation the provider is entitled to, and the provider or any other person may not submit a charge to any other party, including the family of the deceased, for this service.

In the case of a victim of a homicide, the transport service shall be paid the rate stated in §F(1) of this regulation to transport the body from the site of autopsy or examination to a location within Maryland specified by the victim's family.
The Office of the Chief Medical Examiner is responsible for completing the Transportation of Bodies Report for payment.
The Office of the Chief Medical Examiner is responsible for providing a replacement body pouch when the body is transported to that office.

container.heading: Testing Blood and Breath for Alcohol

Effective date: October 4, 2010 (37:20 Md. R. 1394)
Regulations .01 — .09 repealed effective May 11, 2026 (53:9 Md. R. 414)

section.heading: Therapists (Occupational Therapists, Physical Therapists, Speech-Language Pathologists, or Audiologists).

para.text: The Department shall consider an occupational therapist, physical therapist, speech-language pathologist, or audiologist to be professionally licensed if the individual holds:

A valid license issued by the State Board of Occupational Therapy Practice, the State Board of Physical Therapy Examiners, or the State Board of Audiologists, Hearing Aid Dispensers, and Speech-Language Pathologists, in accordance with the relevant provisions of the Health Occupations Article, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
A valid compact privilege to practice in Maryland under an applicable interstate licensure compact to which the State is a party.
This individual does not require an additional educator license.

para.text: Definition. In this regulation, State-rated capacity means the number of students that the IAC or its designee determines that an individual school has the physical capacity to enroll.
Elementary Schools.
For the purposes of determining State-rated capacity, elementary schools are schools enrolling students in one or more grades from prekindergarten through grade 6, but no grades higher than grade 6.
An elementary-school classroom is a space in which the majority of the school day is spent in instruction of the core curriculum.

Elementary school students who circulate through the school to different classrooms for their core curriculum, rather than spending the majority of the school day in a single classroom and leaving the classroom only for special courses (art, music, physical education, and similar), should be considered middle school students for the purposes of calculating State-rated capacity.

For the purposes of measuring utilization of facilities and allocating State capital dollars for projects in a manner most equitable to the local school systems of the State, the IAC shall count capacity of elementary school classrooms as follows:
Prekindergarten classroom — 20;
Kindergarten classroom — 22;
Grades 1 through 5 classroom — 23;
Grade 6 classroom:
If located in an elementary school—23; or
If located in an elementary/middle school, or a secondary school—25;
Special education classroom—10; and
Alternative education classroom—10.
State rated capacity for an individual elementary school is calculated according to the following formula:
Multiply the number of classrooms in each grade by the approved capacity for that grade; and
Then add the resulting products.
Open-space classroom capacity is calculated according to the following formula:
Divide the open space area by a square foot number the IAC determines;
Next, round the quotient to the nearest whole number; and
Then, multiply the rounded quotient by the State-approved capacity for the elementary grade.
Secondary Schools.
For purposes of determining State-rated capacity, secondary schools are schools enrolling students in one or more grades from 6 through 12.
Classrooms.
A secondary-school classroom is a space in which any scheduled instruction takes place.

Secondary-school classrooms include but are not limited to laboratories, career technology rooms, music rooms, art rooms, consumer science rooms, gymnasiums, and auxiliary physical education classrooms. One physical education classroom up to 2,500 net square feet which contains specialized equipment hat cannot be stored or relocated may be excluded in facilities functioning as a high school serving grades 9—12, or a middle high school serving grades 6—12, upon application to and approval of the IAC or its designee.

Auxiliary gymnasiums up to 6,000 net square feet count as one teaching station. A gymnasium over 6,000 net square feet and up to 13,000 net square feet counts as two classrooms. A gymnasium exceeding 13,000 net square feet counts as three classrooms.

For the purposes of measuring utilization of facilities and allocating State capital dollars for projects in a manner most equitable to the local school systems of the State, the IAC shall count capacity of secondary school classrooms as follows:
Grades 6 through 12 classroom—25;
Special education classroom—10; and
Alternative education classroom—10.
State-rated capacity for an individual secondary school is calculated according to the following formula:
For regular classrooms:
Multiply the number of regular classrooms by the approved capacity;
Next, add the resulting products; and
Then, multiply this sum by 85 percent;
For special education classrooms, multiply the number of special education classrooms by the approved capacity; and
Then, add results from §C(4)(a) and (b) of this regulation.
Open-space classroom capacity is calculated according to the following formula:
Divide the open space area by a square foot number the IAC determines;
Next, round the quotient to the nearest whole number;
Then, multiply the rounded quotient by the State-approved capacity for the secondary grade; and
Finally, multiply by 85 percent.
Career and Technology Programs.
Career and technology programs are occupational programs approved by the Maryland State Department of Education.
The approved capacity for a career and technology classroom is as the IAC or its designee determines on a case-by-case basis.
State-rated capacity for an individual career and technology program is calculated according to the following formula:
Multiply the number of classrooms by the approved capacity for that career and technology classroom;
Next, add the resulting products; and
Then, multiply by 85 percent.
The IAC or its designee shall determine on a case-by-case basis the State-rated capacity for a school that is not defined in §§B, C, and D of this regulation.

Cooperative use space dedicated in a written agreement to use by a non-LEA entity may not be included in the State-rated capacity unless that space is used for LEA educational purposes during the LEA’s standard school operating hours.
The State may fund eligible costs of approved public school construction projects according to the State cost share percentage established in this regulation.
Percentages.
The minimum State share of public school construction funding for eligible costs of approved projects is 50 percent.
Reductions in the cost share may not exceed 5 percent.

Reduction in the cost share of 4 percent or more shall be phased in over the 2-year period with a reduction of 2 percentage points in the first year and the remaining 2 or 3 percentage points in the second year.
The maximum State share of public school construction funding is 100 percent of eligible costs of approved projects.
The State share percentage for the Maryland School for the Blind shall be 93 percent of eligible costs of approved projects.
Revisions to Percentages.

By October 2018 and every 2 years thereafter, the IAC shall update, approve, and publish to the IAC’s website the cost share percentage to be applied to projects submitted for approval in the Fiscal Year.
The IAC shall use the formula in §D of this regulation to recommend revisions to the State cost share percentage for each county.
LEA State Cost Share. The IAC shall add the following amounts to calculate the recommended revised cost share amounts:
The LEA's current State share of the Foundation program divided by the Foundation program of the LEA as defined under Education Article, §5-202, Annotated Code of Maryland;

The current amount of State aid provided to the LEA by the guaranteed tax base program as defined under Education Article, §5-210, Annotated Code of Maryland, divided by the Foundation program of the LEA;
20 percent of the amount by which the LEA's free and reduced price meal percentage exceeds the Statewide free and reduced price meal percentage in the prior school year;

5 percent if the county where the LEA is located is a Tier I county that has an unemployment rate greater than the average rate or number of percentage points identified in Economic Development Article, §1-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, or has met the average rate or number of percentage points identified in Economic Development Article, §1-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, at some time during the preceding 24-month period;

5 percent if the county where the LEA is located is a Tier I county that has a median household income level below the level identified in Economic Development Article, §1-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, or has had a median household income level below the level identified in Economic Development Article, §1-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, at some time during the preceding 24-month period;

The difference between the percent growth in the LEA's full-time equivalent enrollment, as defined by Education Article, §5-202, Annotated Code of Maryland, from the 6th prior year to the prior year, and the percent growth in the Statewide full-time equivalent enrollment from the 6th prior year to the prior year, provided this calculation results in a positive number; and

10 times the amount by which the county's and local board’s total outstanding school construction debt at the end of the 2nd prior fiscal year plus the county's total school construction expenditures from its operating budget from the 4th to the 2nd prior fiscal years exceeds 1 percent of the county wealth, as defined by Education Article, §5-202, Annotated Code of Maryland, for the prior fiscal year.
Project Based Add-Ons to State Cost Share. The IAC shall add the following amounts to an LEA’s State Cost share of a proposed school construction project as applicable:

10 percentage points if the proposed school construction project, when the LEA submits the project for approval to the IAC, is at a school with a concentration of poverty level, as defined in Education Article, §5-223, Annotated Code of Maryland, of 80 percent or greater;

5 percentage points if the proposed school construction project, when the LEA submits the project for approval to the IAC, is at a school with a concentration of poverty level, as defined in Education Article, §5-223, Annotated Code of Maryland, of less than 80 percent but greater than 55 percent;
5 percentage points if the proposed school construction project is at a school that, in the most recent school maintenance effectiveness assessment by the IAC, received an assessment rating of:
Good;
Superior; or
Adequate and the school's most recent school facility assessment average percentage of expected useful lifespan is at least 120 percent; and
5 percentage points if the proposed school construction project is a net-zero school.
For FY 2025 and 2026, the State share percentages of public school construction funding for eligible costs of approved projects are as follows:

section.heading: Changes to the Maximum State Construction Allocation.
section.text: After the IAC sets the maximum State construction allocation in the State capital improvement program:

para.text: The IAC may reduce the maximum State construction allocation if a local board changes a board decision which served as justification for setting the approved scope of the project.
The IAC may increase the maximum State construction allocation for a capital maintenance project when:
The LEA submits:
The final project scope of work; and
A cost estimate developed by a design professional licensed in the State of Maryland or the bid tabulation for the project;
The LEA documents that the:
LEA has taken reasonable actions to reduce construction costs; and
Construction costs have resulted from exceptional circumstances; and
The IAC determines that:
The requested scope of work is eligible for State funding; and
The scope of work and associated costs are reasonable.
The IAC may increase the maximum State construction allocation for a project other than a capital maintenance project if the LEA documents that the:
Project has critical impact on the LEA's budget;
LEA has taken reasonable actions to reduce construction costs; and
Construction costs that have resulted from exceptional circumstances or circumstances that are different from those that confront most projects in the State or region.

The IAC may increase the maximum State construction allocation for a project that qualifies for the net zero add-on as described in COMAR 14.39.02.06E(4) after the establishment of the maximum State construction allocation upon submission of acceptable documentation to become eligible for the add-on, and only up to the amount that the project would have been eligible for had the net zero add-on been in place at the time of establishment of the maximum State construction allocation.
If, within 2 years after a project is approved for planning, no part of the project is under contract, the IAC may determine the project to be abandoned and rescind the planning approval.
An LEA that seeks reapproval of a project whose planning approval has been rescinded shall submit a new planning request in a future local capital improvement program.
If, within 2 years after funding is made available for a project, no part of the project is under contract, the IAC shall determine that the project is abandoned and rescind the funding approval.

When the IAC rescinds funding approval, the IAC shall reserve the funding for another eligible project in the county in the current fiscal year or for eligible projects in the county in the next fiscal year.

Funds reserved for a county that have not been used to place a project under contract within 2 years of the date the funds were reserved shall be available for any project approved in a future State capital improvement program.
An LEA that seeks reapproval of a project whose funding approval has been rescinded shall submit a new funding request in a future local capital improvement program.

Regulation .33 adopted effective July 7, 2025 (52:13 Md. R. 656):

para.text: All projects shall comply with State procedures and requirements for educational and design review.
A county board is encouraged, consistent with competitive bidding, to use bulk purchasing, bundling, and intergovernmental purchasing.
Project Delivery Methods. The requirements of COMAR 14.39.04 apply to procurements conducted in accordance with this chapter.

Minority Business Enterprise (MBE). The State’s minority business enterprise program applies to procurements conducted in accordance with this chapter that the State funds in whole or part. Any LEA utilizing State funding for school construction projects shall;
Adopt an MBE policy and procedures guiding document;
Provide the LEA’s board-adopted MBE policy and any related guiding documents to the IAC annually by a date specified by the IAC; and
Certify that all MBE policies are in compliance with the State’s MBE program.
Prevailing Wage. To the extent required by law, the State's prevailing wage requirements apply to procurements conducted in accordance with this chapter that the State funds in whole or part.
Debriefing and Protests.

The LEA shall establish procedures by which unsuccessful bidders and offerors may be debriefed upon written request within a reasonable time by a procurement official familiar with the rationale for the selection decision and contract award.
The LEA shall establish procedures by which an interested party may file a protest relating to the solicitation or award of a procurement contract.

If a single bid or offer is submitted following appropriate notification and solicitation procedures, the LEA may award the contract to the single bidder or offeror if the bid or offer is determined responsible and responsive.
